For VCC 1287 we obtain a velocity dispersion of 24 km s ^ 1 in comparison to the published value of 33 ^ +16 _ 10 km s ^ 1 , while for DF44 we obtain 43 km s ^ 1 in comparison to the published value of 47 ^ +8 _ 6 km s ^ 1 . Our estimates are within 1 of the published results.

Finally, we have also included the Virgo galaxy, VCC 1287, for comparison in Figure fig:mass . Our estimate for the enclosed mass within r_e for this galaxy is 1.3 10^9 M _ , lower than the published estimate 2.6^ +3.4 _ 1.3 10^9 M _ ; beasleya but just within 1 of that value.

sources.summary: VCC1287 is treated as a Virgo ultra-diffuse galaxy with 22 globular clusters, dwarf-like stellar mass, and a GC-to-total-mass ratio consistent with the sample.

quotes.quote: The first is VCC1287 in the Virgo cluster. It has 22 8 GCs and total GC system mass of around 2.2 0.8 x 10 ^6 2016ApJ...819L..20B . The galaxy stellar mass is 2.8 10^7 .

VCC1287 has a galaxy total mass, based on the motions of its GCs, of M_ 200 = 8 4 10^ 10 2016ApJ...819L..20B . This implies a GC system to total mass ratio of 5.5 10^ 5 , which is consistent with the other galaxies in this plot.

sources.summary: VCC 1287 is a Virgo UDG with prior kinematic work, unavailable line-index measurement here, published SED-fitting populations, and a very-early-infall phase-space location.

quotes.quote: Details about each observation can be found in the original papers presenting the dynamical and kinematic analysis of those UDGs: the Virgo cluster UDG VCC 1287 Gannon2020G , NGC 5846-UDG1 in the NGC 5846 group Forbes2021 , UDG1137+16 from the UGC 6594 group Gannon2021 , several UDGs in the Perseus cluster (i.e., PUDG-R15, PUDG-S74, PUDG-R84 and PUDG-R24; Gannon2022 ), and Yagi358 in the Coma cluster Gannon2022b . While this does not have any impact on the full spectral fitting procedure detailed below, it means that we can not perform a line index measurement for VCC 1287, NGC 5846-UDG1, UDG1137+16, PUDG-R24 and PUDG-S74. VCC 1287, PUDG-R24, Yagi358, DFX1 and DF07 have published SED fitting stellar populations (e.g., Pandya2017 ; Buzzo2022 ).

9 UDGs (PUDG-R27, VCC 1287, Yagi358, Yagi093, Yagi098, Yagi276, Yagi392, Yagi418 and OGS1) are located in the `very early infall' region, with other 2 (DF07 and PUDG-R15) in the `early infall' region.

gmw_00002359.name: 3C 63
gmw_00002359.overview: 3C 63 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:1903.10768] that exhibits a possible X-shaped radio morphology [arXiv:1210.6027]. Its emission-line region is comparable in size to its double-lobed radio source, featuring gas aligned with the radio axis and an arc-like structure wrapping around the southern lobe [arXiv:1903.10768].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 63 as an FR II radio galaxy with transverse optical emission, possible X-shaped radio morphology, and nuclear plus diffuse X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 63 is an FR II radio galaxy that lies at redshift 0.175. Baum et al. (1988) report on faint emission regions transverse to the radio axis, particularly in the western region, together with the presence of an S-shaped filament in the optical images. Recently, Cheung et al. (2007) have suggested that the radio morphology is that of an ``X-shape" radio source. In the X-rays we detect only the nucleus. There appears to be diffuse X-ray emission around the nucleus up to a radius 2.5 .

sources.summary: MUSE shows 3C 063 has an emission-line region comparable to its double-lobed radio source, including gas aligned with the radio axis and an arc around the southern lobe.

quotes.quote: 3C 063: FR II/HEG, 1 = 2.99 kpc. The size of the emission line region is similar to that of this double-lobed radio source. On the east side the gas extends along PA 80^ for 8 ( 25 kpc) with a small velocity gradient. On the opposite side, emission lines extend initially toward the South-West, out to 10 , well aligned with the radio axis. At the location of the southern hot spot, the gas sharply bend toward the North, forming an arc-like structure, located at a distance of 33 kpc from the nucleus, wrapping around the southern lobe.

gmw_00002360.name: 3C 79
gmw_00002360.overview: 3C 79 is a Fanaroff-Riley II high-excitation radio galaxy [arXiv:0712.4145, arXiv:2010.11195] hosted by a massive elliptical galaxy with a compact companion [arXiv:0712.4145]. It features a luminous, X-shaped extended emission-line region [arXiv:2010.11195] that is photoionized by a hidden active nucleus [arXiv:0712.4145, arXiv:1210.6027].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 79 as an FR II radio galaxy with a massive elliptical host, compact companion, and luminous EELR whose photoionized low-metallicity gas resembles quasar EELRs.

quotes.quote: We present extensive ground-based spectroscopy and \ imaging of 3C 79, an FR II radio galaxy associated with a luminous extended emission-line region (EELR). Surface brightness modeling of an emission-line-free \ R band image reveals that the host galaxy is a massive elliptical with a compact companion 0 8 away and 4 magnitudes fainter. The host galaxy spectrum is best described by an intermediate-age (1.3 Gyr) stellar population (4 by mass), superimposed on a 10 Gyr old population and a power law ( _ = -1.8 ); the stellar populations are consistent with super-solar metallicities, with the best fit given by the 2.5 \ models. We derive a dynamical mass of 4 10^ 11 \ within the effective radius from the velocity dispersion.

The EELR of 3C 79 is remarkably similar to the most luminous quasar EELRs. The velocity field, although only available in a small area, is locally ordered but globally disordered. The EELR is almost certainly photoionized by the hidden quasar, and it shows densities of 100 \ in the \ emitting regions and temperatures around 13,000 K. Most interestingly, the metallicity of the gas in both the EELR and the NLR is about 1/3 2/3 solar, matching perfectly the metallicity in both the EELRs and the nuclear BLRs of the EELR quasars.

sources.summary: 3C 079 is an FR II/HEG radio galaxy whose extended emission-line region is X-shaped, large, rotating, and accompanied by fainter perpendicular filaments.

quotes.quote: 3C 079: FR II/HEG, z=0.256, 1 = 4.0 kpc (see Fig. 3c079 ). The ELR has an X-shaped morphology. The two brightest and most extended structures ( 80 kpc of total size) are oriented perpendicularly to the radio axis, and show ordered rotation with an amplitude of 800 . In addition, there are several filaments of line emission of similar size but of lower brightness extending perpendicularly to the main emission line structure. The brightest regions of the ELR have higher / \ values than those of lower brightness; no clear trend with distance is seen.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 79 as an FR II high-excitation radio galaxy with a luminous emission-line region photoionized by a hidden active nucleus but no clear extended X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 79 is an FR II radio galaxy associated with a luminous extended emission-line region fu08 , being described as due to photoionization by the hidden active nucleus. It has been also classified as an HEG buttiglione09 . There is no clear detection of extended emission.

sources.summary: The appendix compares K-band luminosity estimates for 3CR 079, noting earlier values disagree and arguing its flat R-K profile disfavors an underestimated nuclear luminosity from the color-profile method.

quotes.quote: 3CR 079 : SWW00 measured L_ K = 10 ^ 43.66 , a factor of 4-6 larger than our measurement, and a factor 3-5 fainter than the one reported in taylor96 . The discrepancy between these measurements is not easily understandable. While the observed flat R-K^ profile excludes a possible underestimate of L_ K^ , the standard technique requires a more uncertain modeling of the host galaxy, possibly causing to underestimate the host contribution.

gmw_00002361.name: 3C 89
gmw_00002361.overview: 3C 89 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy [arXiv:1602.01094, arXiv:2010.11195] with a wide-angle-tail morphology [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:1602.01094] and a weak X-ray active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1602.01094]. It is hosted within a galaxy cluster [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:1602.01094] that features a large X-ray cavity [arXiv:1210.6027] and a 10 kpc linear emission-line structure [arXiv:2010.11195].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 89 as a wide-angle-tail radio galaxy embedded in an X-ray-detected cluster, with hot gas between its radio arms and a large surrounding cavity.

quotes.quote: 3C 89 is a nearby wide angle tail radio galaxy with an uncertain optical classification. It is hosted in a galaxy cluster that is clearly detected in the X-rays in our snapshot observations. An interesting aspect is that the brighter parts of the hot gas are between the arms of the radio source. In particular, there is a bright ridge of X-ray emission running back from the nucleus. The nuclear position is best defined by limiting the energy band to 5-7keV.

We note that the cluster of galaxies surrounding 3C 89 has the largest known X-ray cavity: 270 kpc in diameter sun12 . The X-ray images of 3C89 for the energy band 0.5-7 keV, with different resolution are shown in Figure fig:3c89a and fig:3c89b

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C89 as a luminous FR-I radio galaxy and likely dominant galaxy of an infalling subcluster, with a WAT morphology, X-ray tail, weak AGN, and jet-power estimates.

quotes.quote: The luminous FR-I radio galaxy, 3C89, appears to be the cD galaxy of the infalling subcluster. We estimated 3C89's jet power from jet bending and the possible interaction between the X-ray gas and the radio lobes.
The cluster hosts a luminous FR-I radio galaxy, 3C89, which has a wide angle tail (WAT) like morphology.

The galaxy in the north is the radio galaxy 3C89 (marked by BCG 2). The \ data reveal a weak X-ray AGN in the 2 - 8 keV band (section 5). The overlaid X-ray contours indicate a bright tail behind 3C89.

sources.summary: 3C 089 is an FR I radio source with a 10 kpc linear emission-line feature aligned with the western radio jet and a flat velocity field.

quotes.quote: 3C 089: FR I, no optical classification, z=0.138, 1 = 2.46 kpc (see Fig. 3c089 ). A linear feature of line emission emerges along the PA of the western radio jet and reaches a distance of 10 kpc. The velocity field is remarkably flat.

gmw_00002362.name: 3C 130
gmw_00002362.overview: 3C 130 is a classical Fanaroff-Riley class I elliptical radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.1090 [arXiv:astro-ph/9803280, arXiv:1210.6027]. It is situated in a luminous cluster environment and features a pair of symmetric inner jets, one of which terminates in a compact, shock-like hotspot [arXiv:astro-ph/9803280, arXiv:astro-ph/0312245].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA observations of 3C130, describing it as a powerful FRI wide-angle-tail radio galaxy with symmetric inner jets, a compact hot spot, and a luminous cluster environment.

quotes.quote: I present 1.5- and 8.4-GHz observations with all configurations of the NRAO VLA of the wide-angle tail source 3C130 . The source has a pair of relatively symmetrical, well-collimated inner jets, one of which terminates in a compact hot spot. Archival ROSAT PSPC data confirm that 3C 130's environment is a luminous cluster with little sign of sub-structure in the X-ray-emitting plasma. I compare the source to other wide-angle tail objects and discuss the properties of the class as a whole. None of the currently popular models is entirely satisfactory in accounting for the disruption of the jets in 3C 130.

3C130 is a FRI radio source at redshift 0.109 (Spinrad \ 1985). Its 178-MHz luminosity is 7.6 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 sr ^ 1 , slightly above the nominal FRI-FRII boundary of 2 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 sr ^ 1 (Fanaroff Riley 1974, hereafter FR). Leahy (1985, 1993) and J agers and de Grijp (1985) present intermediate-resolution VLA maps of the central regions of the source, while J agers (1983) has a lower-resolution WSRT image which shows the whole source and its field; the source extends for 1.5 Mpc. Saripalli \ (1996) present high-frequency maps made with the Effelsberg 100-m telescope.

sources.summary: The paper presents four-frequency radio observations of 3C 130, modeling its wide-angle-tail jets and plumes with spectral tomography and finding asymmetric high-frequency plume spectra.

quotes.quote: I present four-frequency radio observations of the wide-angle tail radio galaxy 3C 130 . By a technique of simulated observations, I assess the systematic errors in the data due to the varying uv plane coverage in the different observations used. Using spectral tomography I show that, at least in the southern plume, the source can be represented by a two-component model consisting of a flat-spectrum core and steep-spectrum sheath, as recently found in other FRI sources. In addition, there is a strong difference in the high-frequency spectra of the northern and southern plumes. I discuss the implications of these observations for the source's jet dynamics and particle acceleration, and discuss models of the class of WATs as a whole.

In an earlier paper (Hardcastle , hereafter Paper I) I presented new radio maps of the wide-angle tail radio galaxy 3C 130 ( z=0.109 ). Two-frequency spectral index mapping in that paper showed flat-spectrum jets (in this paper, the term is reserved for the narrow, well-collimated features seen in the inner 50 kpc of the sources) and a hotspot (a compact, sub-kpc feature at the end of the northern jet) together with steeper-spectrum material at the edges of the plumes (the broader, more diffuse features seen between 50 and 500 kpc from the nucleus). This steep-spectrum material is particularly clear in the southern plume, and is referred to here as a `sheath', although I emphasise that, unlike the sheaths seen in some twin-jet FRI radio galaxies, this region has no polarization properties to distinguish it from the rest of the plume.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 130 as a classical FR I elliptical radio galaxy whose X-ray core is detected with significant extended emission near the center.

quotes.quote: 3C 130 is a classical FRI radio galaxy at redshift 0.1090. The optical morphology is clearly elliptical without any peculiarity. The core is detected in the X-rays and it also shows significant extended X-ray emission around its central region.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 130 as a WAT comparison object whose northern jet ends in a compact sub-kpc feature interpreted as shock-like terminal hotspot behavior.

quotes.quote: However, Hardcastle (1998) showed that the WAT 3C 130 ( z=0.109 ) exhibits a bright, compact, sub-kpc feature at the termination of the N jet. The similarity of this feature to the terminal hotspots in FRIIs led Hardcastle (1998) to suggest that 3C 130's N jet also terminated in a shock; further multi-frequency observations suggest that the absence of a comparable feature in the S plume is reflected in the source's particle acceleration properties (Hardcastle 1999). These observations motivated us to carry out a comparably detailed study of a larger sample of WATs, with the aim of determining the properties of the inner jets and the nature of the jet termination in the WAT population as a whole.

gmw_00002363.name: 3C 166
gmw_00002363.overview: 3C 166 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy optically classified as a low-excitation radio galaxy [arXiv:1210.6027]. It features an unresolved infrared nucleus and a radio structure with two lobes of very different morphologies [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:0712.0595].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 166 as an FR II low-excitation radio galaxy with unusual mismatched lobes, a bright infrared nucleus, and no detected extended X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 166 : is an FR II radio galaxy optically classified as LEG buttiglione09 . It shows an unusual radio structure featuring two lobes with very different morphologies spangler82 . The source has a bright unresolved infrared nucleus floyd08 . We did not detect any extended emission in the X-ray.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 166 as an unusual two-lobed radio source whose north-south radio axis has southern infrared hotspots and a clearly detected unresolved infrared nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3C 166; z=0.245 An unusual radio source featuring two lobes with very different morphologies spanglerbridle82 . The radio axis runs north-south, with Spangler Bridle's southern features C and D detected on the IR image as infrared hotspots. The source has a clearly-detected, unresolved infrared nucleus.

gmw_00002364.name: 3C 180
gmw_00002364.overview: 3C 180 is a Fanaroff-Riley II high-excitation radio galaxy [arXiv:2010.11195, arXiv:1210.6027] hosted by a giant elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1210.6027]. Its extended emission-line region spans 45 kiloparsecs on both sides of the nucleus [arXiv:2010.11195], displaying rotating structures [arXiv:2010.11195] and gas ionized at the edges of its radio lobes [arXiv:1811.04946].

sources.summary: 3C 180 is an FR II/HEG whose arc-like ELR extends 45 kpc on both sides, roughly follows the radio axis, rotates, and shows local fluctuations.

quotes.quote: 3C 180: FR II/HEG, z=0.220, 1 = 3.58 kpc (see Fig. 3c180 ). A series of arc-like features is seen on both sides of the nucleus, confirming the structure already seen in the HST images baldi19 . The ELR extends by 45 kpc on both sides and it is oriented along a similar angle of the radio axis (the radio image used, the only available high resolution image from the NRAO VLA Archive Survey Images, is at a frequency of 8.4 GHz and only shows the two hot spots). In general, the ELR shows a rotating structure with a full amplitude of 700 , but with local velocity fluctuations on the larger scales. The velocity dispersion shows an increase at the edges of the brightest arcs.

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 180 as a spectacular HEG with extended tails, bridges, shells and halos, strong filamentary line-ratio peaks, and gas ionized at radio-lobe edges.

quotes.quote: Spectacular are the cases of 3C 33 and 3C 180. Their extended emission-line regions (ELRs) show tails, bridges, shells, irregular features and amorphous halos.
3C 180 shows line ratios of up to 8 spread through the filaments of emission observed 2-5 (7-18 kpc) to the north and south of the nucleus.
In the case of our emission maps, the region of multiple shocks is clear for 3C 180, where at the edges of the radio lobes, the gas is pressured and ionized.

sources.summary: The paper classifies 3C 180 as an FR II high-excitation radio source in a giant elliptical cluster member, but finds no clear X-ray cluster emission around it.

quotes.quote: 3C 180 is a classical FR II radio source optically classified as HEG buttiglione09 . It is hosted in a giant elliptical galaxy madrid06 and according to McCarthy et al. (1995) it is also a member of a cluster of galaxies. We do not detect any clear signatures of X-ray emission arising from the surrounding galaxy cluster.

gmw_00002365.name: 3C 196.1
gmw_00002365.overview: The active galaxy 3C 196.1 is a low-excitation [arXiv:1809.10693, arXiv:1811.04946] FR II radio source [arXiv:1811.04946, arXiv:2010.11195] and the dominant cD galaxy of a cluster [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:1809.10693]. It features a central bi-conical emission structure [arXiv:1811.04946] and an ionized-gas cavity likely inflated by its radio outflow [arXiv:2010.11195].

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 196.1 as an FR II/HYMORS radio galaxy in a bright X-ray cluster, associated with the dominant cD galaxy and showing compact, asymmetric radio structure.

quotes.quote: 3C 196.1 lies within a cluster of galaxies catalogued in the ROSAT bright source catalog and by Kocevski et al. (2007) in their search for clusters in the zone of avoidance. With an exposure of 8ks, there are 3700 net counts detected in a circle of radius 90 . The global temperature estimate for the entire cluster is 4.2 0.2 keV. Restricting the spectral analysis to smaller circles, the temperature drops: 3.4 0.2 for =20 ; 3.0 0.3 for =7 , and 1.0 keV for =1.5 , the last value centered on the brightest part of the inner structure. With a redshift of 0.198, the luminosity distance is 936 Mpc and the angular scale is 3.16 kpc per arcsec.

The host galaxy is a cD, the dominant galaxy of a group of 14 others that lie within about 350 kpc from its core baum88 . Madrid et al. (2006) show that its near-infrared image is elliptical, presenting elongated structure northeast to southwest, which is the same direction as the radio emission. The same morphology is seen in the optical dekoff96,baum88 .

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C 196.1 as a hybrid radio galaxy and BCG, linking jet-aligned gas, X-ray cavities, AGN outbursts, cool-core conditions, and merger signatures.

quotes.quote: We present a multi-wavelength analysis based on archival radio, optical and X-ray data of the complex radio source 3C 196.1, whose host is the brightest cluster galaxy of a z=0.198 cluster. HST data show \ emission aligned with the jet 8.4 GHz radio emission. An \ filament coincides with the brightest X-ray emission, the northern hotspot. Analysis of the X-ray and radio images reveals cavities located at galactic- and cluster- scales. The galactic-scale cavity is almost devoid of 8.4 GHz radio emission and the south-western \ emission is bounded (in projection) by this cavity.

3C 196.1 has been classified in the optical as a low-excitation radio galaxy LERG, buttiglione10 , and is associated with the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG), an elliptical cD galaxy, the dominant galaxy of a group of 14 others that lie within about 350 kpc from its core baum88 . madrid06 show that its near-infrared image is elliptical, presenting an elongated structure NE to SW, in the same direction as the inner scale jet. The same morphology is seen in the optical baum88,dekoff96 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 196.1 as a LEG with H emission extended northeast of the nucleus, central bi-conical emission, a large ELR, and FR II classification.

quotes.quote: For example, the H image of 3C 196.1 shows emission extended 8 kpc to the northeast of the nuclear region which is not observed in the corresponding [ O III ] image. Interesting are the cases of 3C 136.1 and 3C 196.1, which show two bi-conical structures of emission in the central region.

The objects, which show large ELR emitting over 1 kpc, are HEGs, with the exception of one LEG, 3C 196.1. Regarding the HEGs, the H and [ O III ] emitted in the central kpc is roughly half than what is seen in LEGs in the same area. This means that LEGs typically show more compact emission-line region than HEGs. The [ O III ] /H line ratio images The third column in Figure panel depicts the log of the [ O III ] /H emission line ratios for those galaxies with extended emission and/or sufficiently high ( > 5) signal-to-noise (3C 33, 3C 93.1, 3C 136.1, 3C 180, 3C 196.1, 3C 197.1, 3C 219, 3C 234 and 3C 285). These sources are all FR IIs. They are all classified as HEGs (including 2 BLOs) except for one LEG (3C 196.1).

sources.summary: 3C 196.1 is an FR II/LEG double radio source whose similarly oriented ELR encloses the southwest lobe and suggests an ionized-gas cavity inflated by outflow.

quotes.quote: 3C 196.1: FR II/LEG, z=0.198, 1 = 3.30 kpc (see Fig. 3c196.1 ). Its radio structure is best seen in the 5 GHz image from neff95 : it is a double source with a total size of 5 ( 16 kpc) oriented along PA 40 . The ELR has a similar size and orientation and its SW side enshrouds the radio lobe. In this region we detect very high recession velocities, exceeding 500 , and large line widths. These results suggest that we are seeing a cavity of ionized gas inflated by the radio outflow.

gmw_00002366.name: 3C 198
gmw_00002366.overview: 3C 198 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy [arXiv:1003.2467, arXiv:1210.6027] located in a slightly elongated elliptical host galaxy [arXiv:1003.2467]. It is the only low-redshift 3C source where the optical emission lines are dominated by star-forming regions [arXiv:2010.11195], which may be jet-induced [arXiv:2010.11195].

sources.summary: The paper re-evaluates 3C 198 in Abell 115, warns that the historical optical host may be biased, and suggests bent lobes may point to another host.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 700 (GLEAM J082231+055626) can be found in the 3CR catalogue, where it is listed as 3C 198. It is in cluster Abell 115, and the cross-identification provided is a V = 17 mag galaxy Wyndham1966 corresponding to AllWISE J082231.95+055706.8. However, we are wary of historical identifications that may be biased towards the optically-brightest galaxies, hence our use of mid-infrared images (which also show dust-obscured galaxies). During our reassessment of this particular source, we find 4.9-GHz radio contours in the literature, presented by Massaro2012 . They are unable to locate the core in the X-ray, and nor is it detected in the radio. However, the radio image reveals that the lobes of this extended radio galaxy are bent (Figure disagreementoverlays ). This could be due to backflow of plasma towards the south-east, in a similar way as seen for 3C 28 http://www.jb.man.ac.uk/atlas/object/3C28.html, as part of the `Atlas of DRAGNs' database assembled by Leahy1996 . (G4Jy 99; GLEAM J005550+262436). Alternatively, the narrowing of the radio emission could be the inner parts of the lobes, with the host galaxy being nearby and therefore not in line with the major axis of the radio galaxy. If so, this could explain why Buttiglione2010 - who use the existing identification -- find 3C 198 to be the only object (out of 113 3CR sources considered) to have optical emission-lines consistent with star-forming galaxies rather than AGN.

sources.summary: 3C 198 is an FR II source whose extended emission is dominated by star-forming regions, including knots and diffuse structures suggestive of jet-induced star formation.

quotes.quote: 3C 198: FR II/SF, z=0.081, 1 = 1.54 kpc (see Fig. 3c198 ). This is the only 3C source at z<0.3 where the emission lines are dominated by the presence of star forming regions buttiglione10 . The ELR shows both compact knots and diffuse emission, mainly on its SW side where it reaches a distance of 45 kpc. While the bright knot on the E side is cospatial with a nearby galaxy, the remaining compact line features do not have continuum counterparts. All these structures have a spectrum characteristic of star formation, similarly to what is observed on the nucleus, suggestive of jet-induced star formation.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 198 as a faint FR II radio source in a slightly elongated elliptical host with a detectable nucleus, likely brightest in a small group.

quotes.quote: 3C 198 is an FR II radio source at z=0.081 in a slightly elongated elliptical host galaxy with M_H( Host )=-22.55 and a detectable nuclear point source. The IR nucleus and lack of common reference points on both the H band and optical images mean that we were unable to produce a reasonable color-map. This source appears to be the brightest member of a small group (see mchardy74 ).

sources.summary: The paper classifies 3C 198 as an FR II radio galaxy, notes that the radio nucleus could not be located, and measures a weak X-ray source at the optical galaxy position.

quotes.quote: 3C 198 is classified as FR II on the basis of its radio luminosity at 178 MHz, as reported in Spinrad et al. (1985). With the currently available radio data we have been unable to locate a radio nucleus. The data in Table 2 come from measuring a weak X-ray source at the NED position of the optical galaxy.

gmw_00002367.name: 3C 319
gmw_00002367.overview: 3C 319 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy that lacks detectable nuclear X-ray emission [arXiv:1210.6027]. It features a detected northeastern radio hotspot and a faint, extended optical emission-line region [arXiv:1210.6027].

sources.summary: The paper reports 3C 319 as the only current-sample FR II radio galaxy without nuclear X-ray emission, while detecting its NE hotspot and faint optical emission-line structure.

quotes.quote: 3C 319 is an FR II radio galaxy, the only source in our current sample for which we did not detect X-ray emission from the nucleus. The NE hotspot is detected with 4 counts. The galaxy is also very faint in the HST optical images with an extended emission line region dominated by the [ O III ] line that is more compact than the H + [ N II ] one tremblay09 .

gmw_00002368.name: 3C 357
gmw_00002368.overview: 3C 357 is a giant elliptical Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1210.6027] that hosts active core emission [arXiv:1210.6027]. It features filamentary dust lanes southwest of its nucleus [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1210.6027] and optical emission-line gas extending along its radio axis [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1210.6027].

sources.summary: 3CR 357 has a color-gradient-dependent K-band luminosity estimate, visible dust lanes in the HST image, and tests showing the modeled dust-free profile gives similar R-K behavior.

quotes.quote: 3CR 357 : Assuming a flat outer color profile, we estimated L_ K^ = 10 ^ 42.63 0.21 ; modeling the outer ( 0 4) galaxy profile, we obtained L_ K^ = 10 ^ 42.19 0.34 , 0.44 dex smaller than the former estimate. This object and 3CR 379.1 are the worst ones, with L_ K^ badly constrained (but still within an uncertainty of a factor 3).

In the HST image, extended dust lanes are clearly visible within the galaxy, down to the very inner region. In order to test the sensitivity of our analysis to extended dust lanes, we modeled the galaxy in the HST image with an elliptical model (masking out all the visible dust lanes). Then we repeated our analysis using the `dust-free' modeled galaxy profile: the two R-K^ profiles are very similar (differences well within the error bars).

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 357 as an FR II radio galaxy with offset hotspots, emission-line gas along the radio axis, filamentary dust lanes, elliptical isophotes, and nearby companions.

quotes.quote: 3C 357; z=0.166 3C 357 is an FR II radio galaxy with angular size 90 (250 kpc) at a PA 110 degrees fanti+87,harvanekhardcastle98 . In both lobes the hotspots are off to one side suggesting there has been a change in the jet direction. There is emission line gas extending a few arcsec from the nucleus along the radio axis mccarthy+95 . dekoff+96 and capetti+00b note that there are filamentary dust lanes southwest of the nucleus. The galaxy isophotes are very elliptical with no remarkable features.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 357 as a large FR II low-excitation radio galaxy with aligned optical emission, southwestern dust lanes, a giant elliptical host, and detected core emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 357 is an FR II - LEG radio galaxy at redshift 0.1662 with a projected angular size corresponding to 250 kpc fanti97,harvanek98 . It shows an optical emission line region of a few arcsec scale extending from the nucleus along the radio axis (McCarthy et al. 1995). Both de Koff et al. (1996) and Capetti et al. (2000) reported on filamentary dust lanes in the southwestern region. The host galaxy is a classical giant elliptical with no remarkable features floyd08 . In our \ snapshot observation we clearly detected the core emission but no extended radiation around the core or associated with the hotspots has been observed.

gmw_00002369.name: 3C 402
gmw_00002369.overview: 3C 402 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio source hosted by a smooth, elongated elliptical galaxy with red optical-infrared colors [arXiv:1003.2467]. The system is a confusing double galaxy containing two bright nuclei that are both detected in radio and X-ray wavelengths [arXiv:1210.6027].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 402 as a confusing two-galaxy radio source, with both nuclei detected in radio and X-rays and a possible southwest X-ray jet from 402N.

quotes.quote: 3C 402 There are two bright galaxies separated by 2 ^ in declination and each appears to be a radio galaxy. We suspect that the large scale radio structures are superposed. Both nuclei are detected in radio and X-rays and both are somewhat extended. With the radio maps available to us, our registration of the X-ray map is less secure than usual. This is because of the difficulty of determining the location of the peak brightness for an extended feature and because the radio maps have a large beam size.
The X-ray nucleus of 402N has a 2 \ long extension to the SW, which could be a jet.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 402 as one of the abnormally dim FR II hosts, specifically a smooth elongated elliptical with red optical-infrared colors.

quotes.quote: In addition, seven of the lower redshift sources are worth considering: 3C 105, 3C 198, 3C 227, 3C 326, 3C 353, 3C 402 and 3C 445 are all FR II radio sources in abnormally dim galaxies.

3C 402 is an FR II radio source hosted by a smooth, elongated elliptical galaxy with M_H( Host )=-22.94 at z=0.023 . The galaxy is found to be quite red, with V-H=3.0 on average, rising to around 3.5 in the central regions.

gmw_00002370.name: 3C 410
gmw_00002370.overview: 3C 410 is a Fanaroff-Riley II broad-line radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.2485 [arXiv:1210.6027]. The system features a strong central nuclear point source embedded within a round elliptical host galaxy [arXiv:0712.0595].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 410 as an FR II broad-line radio galaxy whose X-ray nucleus required an unusually large registration shift to align with the central radio component.

quotes.quote: 3C 410 is an FR II broad line radio galaxy buttiglione09 with a redshift of 0.2485. The X-ray nucleus has to be shifted by 1 \ in declination in order to align the central component of the radio triple with the X-ray. In our experience in registering well over a hundred \ observations, the usual shift required to align radio and X-ray nuclei is between 0.2 \ and 0.3 , and almost never larger than 0.4 \ (see also Section sec:obs ). However, since the absolute pointing determined by the Chandra star trackers is occasionally off by 1^ (T. Aldcroft, personal communication), we believe the registration performed is correct.

sources.summary: The paper notes that 3C 410 has noisy two-image NICMOS data, but still shows a strong central nuclear point source in a round elliptical host and many unresolved field sources.

quotes.quote: 3C 410; z=0.248 Our observations of 3C 410 suffered a pointing error, resulting in only two usable images of the source -- hence the rather noisy image. A strong central nuclear point source is visible centered on a round elliptical host galaxy. Numerous unresolved sources are visible in the field.

gmw_00002371.name: 3C 436
gmw_00002371.overview: 3C 436 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II high-excitation radio galaxy [arXiv:1710.01078, arXiv:1210.6027]. It is hosted by an elliptical galaxy that is elongated in the same direction as its radio structure, which includes a primary radio hotspot near the southern lobe [arXiv:1210.6027].

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C436 to test its resolved emissivity model, fitting the radio source properties and explaining why JP spectral ages are younger than its dynamical age.

quotes.quote: In particular, we reproduce the spectral age distribution of 3C436 observed by Harwood+2013 . The intrinsic properties of 3C436 are fitted using the Bayesian algorithm of Turner+2016 based upon multi-frequency luminosity observations Laing+1980 with the lobe size and axis ratio taken from the 8.4 GHz FITS file Hardcastle+1997 . The host environment of 3C436 is modelled here using semi-analytic galaxy evolution SAGE Croton+2006 models informed by the host stellar mass ( M_ = 10^ 11.8 M_ ).

The multi-frequency surface brightness of 3C436 is calculated using the resolved emissivity model for the best fit jet power, source age and magnetic field strength. The radio SED arising from the synchrotron-emitting electrons at a given distance from the hotspot is fitted using the JP model.

Specifically the model can reproduce: (1) the 608 MHz surface brightness map of 3C31, and (2) the spatial distribution of spectral ages in 3C436 inferred from X-ray observations. We find that the oldest electron ages measured by fitting the JP model to the spectra across the 3C436 radio lobes are a factor of a few younger than its dynamical age.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 436 as an FR II high-excitation radio galaxy in an elongated elliptical host, with hard nuclear counts and a detected primary radio hotspot.

quotes.quote: 3C 436 is a typical FR II - HEG hosted in an elliptical galaxy elongated in the same direction as its radio structure madrid06 . For the nucleus, 25 of the 41 total counts lie between 4 and 6 keV. We detect what might be described as the 'primary' radio hotspot close to the tip of the S lobe.

gmw_00002372.name: NGC 7769
gmw_00002372.overview: NGC 7769 is a grand-design spiral galaxy featuring two symmetric long outer arms and flocculent inner arms [arXiv:1510.00193]. It is interacting with NGC 7771, which has induced a region of intensive star formation and weakly distorted NGC 7769's rotation curve [arXiv:1512.00634, arXiv:1510.00193].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 7769 has a weakly distorted rotation curve and likely passed first pericenter with NGC 7771, supporting an interacting-quartet interpretation.

quotes.quote: We found that the rotation curve of NGC 7769 is weakly distorted. The rotation curve of NGC 7771 is strongly distorted with the tidal arms caused by direct flyby of NGC 7769 and flyby of a smaller neighbor NGC 7770.
The rotation curves and morphological disturbances suggest that the NGC 7769 and NGC 7771 have passed the first pericenter stage, however, probably the second encounter has not happened yet.

Maximal rotational velocity of NGC 7769 is observed at the radius of around 15 arcsec from the galaxy nucleus. The rotational velocities in Figure vrot_velr are in good agreement with the HI measurements ( 316 km s^ 1 ) in 1993ApJ...419...30C . Our measurements of velocities, having a better spatial resolution compared with those of the previous studies ( 1993ApJ...419...30C,1997AJ....114...77N ), reveal weak perturbations of the rotation curve of NGC 7769, which may be caused by interaction with NGC 7771.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 7769 weakly distorted by interaction with NGC 7771, with Type III surface-brightness structure and interaction-induced star formation.

quotes.quote: We found that the rotation curve of NGC 7769 is weakly distorted.
Profiles of surface brightness of NGC 7769 have a characteristic break, and profiles of color indices have a minimum at a radius of intensive star formation induced by the interaction with NGC 7771.

The structure of spiral arms of NGC 7769 can be classified as having an arm-class 7 (grand-design) according to the scheme in 1987ApJ...314....3E : two symmetric long outer arms and flocculent inner arms.

gmw_00002373.name: NGC 7771
gmw_00002373.overview: NGC 7771 is an almost edge-on luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:1004.1364] featuring a long bar [arXiv:1510.00193] and a nuclear star-forming ring [arXiv:1004.1364]. It is part of an interacting quartet [arXiv:1810.02371], displaying tidal arms and a distorted rotation curve from encounters with its neighbors [arXiv:1512.00634, arXiv:1510.00193].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7771 as strongly distorted by tidal arms from encounters with NGC 7769 and NGC 7770, with asymmetric radial-velocity structure and first-pericenter evidence.

quotes.quote: The rotation curve of NGC 7771 is strongly distorted with the tidal arms caused by direct flyby of NGC 7769 and flyby of a smaller neighbor NGC 7770. The rotation curve of NGC 7770 is significantly skewed because of the interaction with much massive NGC 7771. The rotation curves and morphological disturbances suggest that the NGC 7769 and NGC 7771 have passed the first pericenter stage, however, probably the second encounter has not happened yet.

The same cannot be said about the velocity field of NGC 7771. Figure vrot_velr shows that there are perturbations and large dispersion in radial velocities at the distances larger than about 10-15 arcsec from the nuclei. This distance is about half radius of the bar. Evidently, this scatter of radial velocities can be explained by the fact that part of the arms are included in the sector used to calculate radial velocities (sector angle is 40^ ). However the asymmetric profile along the major axis suggests that Northern and Southern arms do not have the same radial velocity profiles.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7771 as a grand-design spiral with tidal arms, a long bar, distorted rotation, and interactions with NGC 7769 and NGC 7770.

quotes.quote: Spiral arms of NGC 7771 have arm-class 12 (grand-design) according to the scheme in 1987ApJ...314....3E : two symmetric long arms dominating in the disk. Radio observations in 1997AJ....114...77N and 2005A&A...442..137N showed that the southern arm of NGC 7771 extends much further than what is visible in the optical image, making a bridge to NGC 7771A to the West and ending with a region of bright radio emission, see Figure syste . The northern arm extends up to about 10-15 optical radii of the galaxy (16 arcmin) and becomes an arc stretched around NGC 7770 to the South. The long bar (21 kpc) of NGC 7771 also stands out. The spiral arms of NGC 7771, as well as outer arms of NGC 7769 have tidal origin (e.g. 1972ApJ...178..623T,1979ApJ...233..539K,2011MNRAS.414..538K ).
The same cannot be said about the velocity field of NGC 7771.
Figures velr and vrot show that there are perturbations and large dispersion in radial velocities at distances larger than 10-15 arcsec from the nuclei.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7771 as an almost edge-on LIRG with a nuclear star-forming ring, visible spiral arms in line maps, and an eastern-arm region 6 kpc from the nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 7771 The nucleus of this LIRG is classified as like from optical spectroscopy. It has a nuclear ring of star formation clearly seen in the Pa images (Figure fig_map_sh , see also AAH06s and AAH09PMAS ), but it is not resolved by the IRS observations. This galaxy is seen almost edge-on and we can clearly observe the spiral arms in the line emission maps. We detect an \ region in the eastern arm, 20 arcsec (6 kpc) away from the nucleus. The continuum is dominated by the nucleus.

sources.summary: NGC 7771 is the dominant infrared source in an interacting quartet, with nearby companions, faint NGC 7771A, and multiple disk point sources classified as ULXs.

quotes.quote: [ 104 ] NGC 7771 : This galaxy is part of an interacting quartet of galaxies, along with close companion NGC 7770 at an angular distance of 1.1 ', NGC 7771A at 2.8 ' and NGC 7769 at 5.4 ' e.g. YegNaz2016 . About 90 of the IRAS flux originates in NGC 7771, with NGC 7770 being responsible for the remaining 10 and NGC 7769 being resolved as a separate source by IRAS . NGC 7771A is faint in the infrared, remaining undetected at 8 m and above. There is also no detection for this small component in the Chandra data.

sources.summary: The paper gives NGC 7771 extended PMAS coverage, an intermediate-age stellar population signature, and low-EW line ratios inconsistent with simple H II-region models.

quotes.quote: The map of NGC 7771 covers the central 7.7 kpc 4.4 kpc .
The only exception is NGC 7771 that appears to show the presence of an intermediate age (700 Myr-1 Gyr) stellar population.

On the other hand, galaxies like NGC 23, NGC 7591 and NGC 7771 show a significant numbers of spaxels with EW(H )_ em <20 whose line ratios (not corrected for stellar absorption) cannot be explained by the Dopita et al. (2006) H ii region models.

gmw_00002374.name: NGC 7770
gmw_00002374.overview: NGC 7770 is a spiral galaxy featuring a weak bar and disturbed spiral arms [arXiv:1510.00193]. It is undergoing intense star formation and exhibits a significantly skewed rotation curve due to a strong harassing interaction with its more massive neighbor, NGC 7771 [arXiv:1510.00193, arXiv:1512.00634].

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 7770 has a skewed rotation curve, disturbed spiral arms, a weak bar, and intense star formation from interaction with NGC 7771.

quotes.quote: A comparison of the SDSS images of different colors shows that NGC 7770 has a weak but long enough bar (from NE to SW). Spiral arms of NGC 7770 have arm-class 4 according to scheme in 1987ApJ...314....3E : one remarkable distorted arm, and the second arm is much shorter. Because of the low resolution of images, however, in earlier works of 1983Ap.....19..345E and 1997AJ....114...77N , it was concluded that NGC 7770 has two nuclei. However photometric analysis of the SDSS images of five colors shows that NGC 7770 has seven bright HII regions with intensive star formation, some of which have brightness comparable with the brightness of the nucleus of the galaxy. Both intensive star formation and significant disturbances of arms are caused by the interaction with more massive (about 8 times) neighbor NGC 7771 (e.g. 2007A&A...468...61D ).
The rotation curve of NGC 7770 is significantly skewed. This is probably because of the strong harassing interaction with the more massive NGC 7771, see 2012MNRAS.425L..46A .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7770 as a smaller neighbor whose rotation curve is significantly skewed by interaction with the more massive NGC 7771 and whose tidal influence affects NGC 7771.

quotes.quote: The rotation curve of NGC 7771 is strongly distorted with the tidal arms caused by direct flyby of NGC 7769 and flyby of a smaller neighbor NGC 7770. The rotation curve of NGC 7770 is significantly skewed because of the interaction with much massive NGC 7771.

The asymmetric tidal forces of NGC 7769 and NGC 7770 affecting on NGC 7771, seem to be a natural cause of that. The rotation curve of NGC 7770 is significantly skewed. This is probably because of the strong harassing interaction with the more massive NGC 7771, see 2012MNRAS.425L..46A .

gmw_00002375.name: CSO 167
gmw_00002375.overview: CSO 167 is a bright quasar with an apparent i-band magnitude of 16.50 [arXiv:1712.01205]. Located at a redshift of z=2.56, the object is positioned close to the stellar locus [arXiv:1712.01205].

sources.summary: The paper describes CSO 167 as a bright lower-redshift quasar without raised quality flags, outside the high-redshift regions and too close to the stellar locus for previous SDSS selection.

quotes.quote: (5) CSO 167 CSO 167 is a z=2.56 quasar Sanduleak1984,Everett1995 with m_ i =16.50 . None of the fatal or non-fatal image quality flags are raised. It is not expected to and also does not fall into one of the high redshift inclusion regions. This quasar is too close to the stellar locus (see Fig. fig_cc_missed ) to be selected in previous SDSS campaigns.

gmw_00002376.name: CSO 1061
gmw_00002376.overview: CSO 1061, also known as Q1442+2931, is a bright quasar located at a redshift of z=2.67 [arXiv:1712.01205]. X-ray observations of the active nucleus show a spectrum that fits a power-law model with no evidence of an iron line [arXiv:0801.1692].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Q1442+2931 X-ray data, noting previous power-law results, no Fe-line evidence, and agreement with earlier photon-index and flux-density measurements.

quotes.quote: Q1442+2931. --- An analysis of the \ spectrum of this quasar was presented by Ferrero Brinkmann (2003). They fitted with a power-law and obtained a photon index of 1.87. They found no evidence of an Fe H line. We re-analyzed the same \ data (Obs-ID: 0103060201, PI: Aschenbach). The spectra were binned with a minimum of 50 counts per bin. Our derived values of the photon index and of \ are consistent with those of Ferrero Brinkmann (2003).

sources.summary: The paper treats CSO 1061 as a bright lower-redshift quasar outside the high-redshift inclusion regions, with clean flags but missed because it lies too near the stellar locus.

quotes.quote: (6) CSO 1061 Similar to CSO167, CSO 1061 Sanduleak1989 is at a lower redshift ( z=2.67 ; m_ i =16.33 ) and therefore is not expected to be selected by any of the inclusion regions. None of the fatal or non-fatal image quality flags are raised. This object was also not observed because it is too close to the stellar locus.

gmw_00002377.name: NGC5068
gmw_00002377.overview: NGC 5068 is an isolated, low-mass barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2012.00757, arXiv:2203.02522] located approximately 5.20 Mpc away on the outskirts of the NGC 5236 group [arXiv:1804.00469, arXiv:2012.00757]. It features a patchy, flocculent morphology [arXiv:1910.10520, arXiv:2012.00757] with a young central bar that drives gas inflows to fuel active star formation [arXiv:2212.10751, arXiv:2404.19473].

sources.summary: The paper observes NGC 5068 with HST/ACS, reports its TRGB distance and peculiar velocity, and finds it too separated to be physically connected to DDO 161/UGCA 319.

quotes.quote: Apart from UGCA 319, in the wide surroundings of DDO 161 up to the angular separation of 5^o there are only two galaxies with radial velocities within 200 around the velocity of DDO 161: the dwarf galaxy KK 176 at a separation of 128 arcmin with the velocity of V_ LG = 618 , and the spiral galaxy NGC 5068 at a separation of 310 arcmin with the velocity of V_ LG = 469 . pisano2011 assumed that DDO 161 together with KK 176, NGC 5068 and another dwarf BCD galaxy MCG-03-34-002 ( V_ LG = 765 ) form a loose "HIPASS"-group having the linear diameter of 600 kpc, radial velocity dispersion of 124 and the virial mass of 5.1 2.5 10^ 11 . We have observed the galaxies KK 176 and NGC 5068 with ACS on HST during February 1 and February 11, 2017 in the same program with DDO 161 and UGCA 319. The CM diagrams for both the galaxies are presented in Fig. 3. The estimated values of the TRGB for KK 176 and NGC 5068 as well as basic properties of them are given in Table 3.

The distance estimates: 7.28 0.29 Mpc for KK 176 and 5.16 0.21 Mpc for NGC 5068 do not give grounds for considering these neighbors to be physically connected to the tight pair DDO 161/UGCA 319. The larger galaxy NGC 5068 is 540 kpc removed in projection and 730 310 kpc removed in the line of sight. It is interesting to note that all 4 galaxies with accurately measured distances have positive peculiar velocities, V_ pec = V_ LG H_0 D assuming the Hubble parameter H_0 = 73 , i.e.: +103 (DDO 161), +135 (UGCA 319), +87 (KK 176), and +92 (NGC 5068).

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5068 as a PHANGS-MUSE example, identifying it as the closest low-mass galaxy and showing its resolved SFR-calibration deviations.

quotes.quote: In Fig. fig:fig1 , we show SFR maps for three example galaxies from the PHANGS--MUSE sample. NGC 5068 is a low-mass galaxy and the closest object in our sample (15 = 400 pc). NGC 628 is an archetypal grand-design spiral galaxy, and finally NGC 1566 is a massive barred Seyfert 1 galaxy.

The comparison between \ and FUV+W4 SFR shows that deviations from equality are spatially correlated with the intensity of star formation activity. In particular, the FUV+W4 SFR is biased high in regions of low , while the trend is inverted for regions of high . Such a trend is evident in all three galaxies shown in Fig. fig:fig1 , despite clear differences in the \ distributions, and is also seen in the other PHANGS--MUSE galaxies in our sample.

sources.summary: NGC 5068 has a negative, shallow-steep metallicity gradient, central star-formation increase, and bright bar-region HII regions suggesting bar-driven gas inflow.

quotes.quote: In NGC 5068, we measure a negative metallicity gradient of 0.073 0.002 dex/kpc (left column of Figure fig:Ograd3 ). NGC 5068 is marginally better described with a shallow-steep piecewise-linear function (middle column of Figure fig:Ograd3 ), as the medians of residuals are closer to zero with a piecewise linear fit than a single linear fit, for the regions within R _ bar .

NGC 5068 has a steeply increasing _ SFR from the bar to the center of the galaxy. NGC 1566 and NGC 5068 are best-characterised with a ``shallow-steep'' metallicity gradient. The "steep-shallow" _ SFR of NGC 5068 is consistent with the central star-forming ring smajic_2015 while the metallicity of NGC 5068 is observed as "shallow-steep" which indicates a young bar mixing ISM in the galaxy center. Based on the bright HII regions throughout the bar region, we hypothesise that the bar of NGC 5068 induces gas inflows, fueling the central region.

sources.summary: The paper measures NGC 5068's TRGB distance from peripheral HST/ACS imaging, finding consistency with PNLF distance and placing it on the outskirts of the NGC 5236 group.

quotes.quote: NGC 5068. The HST/ACS images are pointed on the SE periphery of this Scd galaxy to avoid crowded HII-regions. The CMD of NGC 5068 has been presented earlier kar2017a . Here we selected in our measurements the stars with Y < 2066 in the pixel coordinates of the combined distortion-corrected mosaic F606W frame. Our estimate of TRGB distance to it, D = 5.16 0.21 Mpc, is well consistent with the PNLF distance D = 5.45 0.52 Mpc, derived by herr2008 . The galaxy is located on the outskirts of the group around NGC 5236 in Centaurus.

sources.summary: The paper highlights NGC5068 as a low-mass, low-pressure case with weaker CO detections, short feedback timing relative to supernova delay, and the highest inferred feedback coupling.

quotes.quote: With the exception of NGC5068 (which is a low-mass galaxy, with a low-pressure ISM, and relatively low signal-to-noise CO detections), there is little variation between galaxies, with \ ranging between 2.5 Myr and 4.8 Myr.

For galaxy-averaged values, the feedback time-scale is shorter than the typical minimum delay time of the first supernova explosion 4 Myr; e.g. Leitherer14 for five galaxies in our sample (NGC628, NGC3351, NGC3627, NGC4321 and NGC5068).

During the feedback phase (i.e. \ when CO and \ emission coexist), we measure relatively low average coupling efficiencies, ranging between 8 per cent (for NGC4535) and 55 per cent (for NGC5068), for the dominant feedback mechanism (acting on the shortest time-scale) in each galaxy. The uncertainties on the coupling efficiencies are propagated from the 1 uncertainties on the feedback time-scale. We reiterate that we only consider the feedback mechanism with the shortest predicted time-scale here. If several feedback mechanisms interact constructively, the predicted combined feedback time-scale in a given galaxy would be shorter (i.e. \ the offset between the observed and predicted feedback time-scales would increase) and the inferred coupling efficiency would be smaller. The inferred coupling efficiency depends on the time interval over which it is measured. In the left-hand panel, we only consider the feedback phase, during which (part of) the natal cloud is still present. However, if the coupling efficiency is measured over a longer time interval (e.g. \ when interested in the fate of all emitted photons, or of all photons emitted before a certain time), the effective coupling efficiency is lower, because all photons emitted after a time t=t_ fb will escape. It is straightforward to describe this effect analytically. The right panel of Figure fig:efficiency_obs shows the effective coupling efficiency, _ eff , when averaging over the entire lifetime of the \ regions ( , see Table tab:timescales ), which is defined as: _ eff = . The effective coupling efficiency integrated over the entire \ region lifetime ranges between 4 per cent and 17 per cent in our sample. By construction, _ eff is lower than because it takes into account the time during which the cloud is already dispersed, and the coupling is effectively zero. This effect is the most visible for NGC5068, where the coupling is high (55 per cent) during the (short) feedback phase, but drops to 10 per cent when integrating over the \ region lifetime.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5068 as an isolated flocculent spiral and adopts the new PHANGS-HST TRGB distance of 5.20 Mpc.

quotes.quote: NGC 5068 is an isolated flocculent spiral, and the second-nearest PHANGS-HST target, with the existing CMDs/TRGB catalog measurement situating it at D = 5.16 Mpc 0.19 Mpc. The PHANGS-HST field is nicely placed in the outer halo, from which we measure m_ TRGB = 24.57 0.03 mag. This equates to a distance of D = 5.20 Mpc 0.21 Mpc. We choose to adopt the new PHANGS-HST measurement for the distance to NGC 5068, although the two measurements are within less than 1 of each other.

sources.summary: NGC 5068 is treated as a barred spiral with Wolf-Rayet populations, a literature metallicity gradient, and H II-region gradients matching a single linear fit.

quotes.quote: The TYPHOON Survey The six galaxies in this study --- NGC 1566, NGC 2835, NGC 3521, NGC 5068, NGC 5236, and NGC 7793 --- are drawn from the TYPHOON survey. The H signal to noise image of each galaxy are shown in Figure fig:galaxies . The TYPHOON data are constructed using a Progressive Integral Step Method (PrISM), establishing a 3D data cube where each spatial pixel (spaxel) occupies a spectrum for that pixel. A long-slit spectrum is captured and then the slit is moved along spatially, generating multiple slices to form into a cube. Details about the TYPHOON Program and long-slit data reduction techniques will be presented in a forthcoming paper (M. Seibert et al., in preparation). Here, we provide a short summary, focusing on aspects immediately relevant to this study. The TYPHOON galaxies (PI and Team Leader, B.F. Madore) were observed using the Wide-Field CCD imaging spectrograph on the 2.5m du Pont telescope, located at the Las Campanas Observatory in Chile. The WFCCD has a field of view of 25' ; the TYPHOON 3D datacubes are constructed using a custom long-slit (18' × 1.65; 0.5 square arcminute). The slit was positioned as either north-south or east-west, moved after each 600s to scan the data in the perpendicular direction of each galaxy. This was performed until the entire optical disk of each galaxy had been observed. The observations were taken over the course of observing runs from 2011 through 2018. Observations were performed only when the seeing was less than the slit width of 1 '' 65. By choosing nearby galaxies ( z 0.005 ), TYPHOON, and the galaxies in this study, is able to achieve seeing-limited resolutions of up to 5 pc. Standard spectro-photometric flux calibrations were taken each observing night. The data are reduced using standard long-slit data reduction techniques, summarized in ho17 and to be described in detail in a forthcoming paper (M. Seibert et al., in preparation). We briefly summarize here the most important details. The wavelength calibration has a typical root-mean-square value 0.05 \ for the entire data set. Flux calibration is accurate to 2 at the spaxel scale over the range of 4500--7500 . The reduced long-slit 2D spectra are tiled together to form the 3D data cube. The final reduced data cube covers a wavelength range of 3650--8150 , with spectral and spatial samplings of 1.5 \ and 1.65'', respectively. The instrumental dispersion is approximately 3.5 , corresponding to a spectral resolution R 850 at 7000 . We typically reach a 3 surface brightness sensitivity for H of 4.2 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 arcsec ^ 2 . We note that as all main results in this paper (metallicity, ionization, and pressure diagnostics) are inferred from line ratios, the results depend only on the relative flux calibration and are insensitive to the absolute flux calibration. The TYPHOON Galaxies The six galaxies selected for this study are all the spiral galaxies from the TYPHOON survey that have undergone the full data reduction pipeline at the time of writing, and additionally, were not previously published by ho17, ho18 . A summary of properties of the galaxies are listed in Table tab:1 along with the spatial resolution of the observations and the number of \ regions in each galaxy. NGC 1566 The most distant galaxy in the sample at 17.9 Mpc, NGC 1566 is a face-on spiral galaxy with an intermediate-strength bar type, classified as a SAB(s)bc. NGC 1566 has a nuclear gas disk which presents as a star-forming ring smajic15 and an outer pseudo-ring that winds anti-parallel to the bar ends buta15 . NGC 1566 hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nuclei (AGN) that does not show evidence of an outflow nor AGN feedback combes14 . The moderate to loosely wound arms are consistent with being formed through bar-driven spiral density waves shabani18 . The largest \ regions in NGC 1566 of 600 pc (Figure fig:size ) are consistent with the maximum size of star-forming complexes of 508 179 pc as traced by the young stellar population grasha17b . NGC 2835 NGC 2835 is a highly inclined star-forming galaxy with a reported shallow radial metallicity gradient of 0.096 dex/kpc using the dopita16 metallicity calibration kreckel19 . The PHANGS-MUSE data from kreckel19 have a spatial resolution of 51 pc and thus are able to observe \ regions that are 42 smaller than the native resolution of the TYPHOON data. ryder95 use the R_ 23 diagnostic pagel79, kobulnicky04 to report a gradient of 0.041 dex/kpc. pilyugin14 used the data from ryder95 to provide homogeneous abundance determinations for \ regions using T _e based abundances, reporting a negative metallicity gradient of 0.032 dex/kpc. NGC 3521 NGC 3521 is a highly inclined, flocculent spiral galaxy with a barred and inner ring morphology that hosts a stellar counter-rotating component induced by the bar zeilinger01 . There is an \ region at the core of the nucleus that forms a low-ionization nuclear emission-line region, classified as an \ LINER das03 . pilyugin14 reports a negative metallicity gradient of 0.039 dex/kpc using T _e based abundances based less than 12 individual \ regions from zaritsky94, bresolin99 . NGC 5068 NGC 5068 is a barred spiral galaxy with an established population of Wolf-Rayet stars --- helium-burning stars that are descendants of massive O stars with very strong stellar winds. Wolf-Rayet stars, with their spectroscopic signatures, are often serendipitously detected in observations of bright \ regions rosa86 . From a spectroscopic survey of the WR population in NGC 5068, bibby12 derive a metallicity gradient of 0.11 dex/kpc using the pettini04 metallicity calibration.
The running median shows good agreement with the linear fit for NGC 3521, NGC 5068, and NGC 7793, indicating that the gradients are well-described with a single fit in these systems.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5068 as the most active low-mass galaxy in recent star-formation changes and discusses its low-metallicity gas conversion factor.

quotes.quote: NGC 628, 4254, 4303, 4321, 4535, and 5068 belong to the high end of the distribution, while NGC 1365, 1512, 1566, 3351, and 3627 belong to its low end.This is also summarised in the last column of Table tab:yes_no . Looking at the distribution across the galactic disks in the 3 ^ rd column of Figure fig:all_maps , we observe large areas of increasing recent SF in NGC 628, 4254, 4303, and 4321, while NGC 1512, 1566, and 3627 exhibit recent SF activity that ranges from roughly unchanged to decreasing. The most active galaxy in terms of recent SF changes is the low-mass galaxy NGC 5068.

A number of pixels with > 0 are positioned at an intermediate value of _ mol 0.9 M _ pc ^ 2 that belong to the low stellar mass galaxy NGC 5068. Low-mass galaxies do tend to experience a ``burstier'' SF e.g., Weisz_12, Emami_19, flores_velazques_21 , which, however, does not explain the high and SFE at the intermediate _ mol . Instead, we might actually underestimate _ mol in NGC 5068: having lower gas phase metallicity, A(FUV), and A(H ) than the other galaxies in the sample, the assumption of a constant Galactic _ CO =4.35 M _ pc ^ 2 (K km s ^ 1 )^ 1 is likely to be too low.

sources.summary: The paper surveys NGC 5068 for planetary nebulae, reports 19 candidates in the six-galaxy sequence, and presents a first reliable PNLF distance of 5.4 Mpc.

quotes.quote: As the first step to determine disk mass-to-light ratios for normal spiral galaxies, we present the results of an imaging survey for planetary nebulae (PNe) in six nearby, face-on systems: IC 342, M74 (NGC 628), M83 (NGC 5236), M94 (NGC 4736), NGC 5068, and NGC 6946. Using Blanco/Mosaic II and WIYN/OPTIC, we identify 165, 153, 241, 150, 19, and 71 PN candidates, respectively, and use the Planetary Nebula Luminosity Function (PNLF) to obtain distances. For M74 and NGC 5068, our distances of 8.6 0.3 Mpc and 5.4^ +0.2 _ 0.4 Mpc are the first reliable estimates to these objects; for IC 342 ( 3.5 0.3 Mpc), M83 ( 4.8 0.1 Mpc), M94 ( 4.4^ +0.1 _ 0.2 Mpc), and NGC 6946 ( 6.1 0.6 Mpc) our values agree well with those in the literature.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5068 as a low-mass, late-type galaxy with H-alpha-only dominance and patchy, incoherent CO/H-alpha morphology.

quotes.quote: In two targets \ only sightlines dominate (NGC 3351 and NGC 5068).

Here and throughout our analysis, NGC 5068 shows a distinct morphology: a patchy, incoherent distribution of CO and \ emitting regions. This galaxy has the lowest stellar mass, lowest metallicity and latest Hubble type in our sample.

sources.summary: NGC5068 has the sample's lowest average metallicity and behaves as a low-molecular-surface-density outlier, with GMC lifetimes matching internal cloud free-fall or crossing times.

quotes.quote: Over the entire sample, the average metallicity ranges between 12+ ( O / H ) = 8.39 (for NGC5068) and 12+ ( O / H ) = 8.84 (for NGC5194), which translates into a narrow range of associated reference time-scales of t_ star,ref =4.19 4.53 Myr.
Figure fig:radprof shows that in most galaxies (except for NGC3351 and NGC5068) there exists a broad agreement between the measured molecular cloud lifetimes and the analytical predictions.

This regime of `island GMCs' evolving on an internal dynamical time manifests itself e.g. \ in the outskirts of NGC4303 and NGC4535, as well as overall in the low molecular gas surface density galaxies NGC3351 and NGC5068, where the measured \ is consistent with the cloud free-fall time or the cloud crossing time.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5068 as an outlier with low mean virial parameter, isolated CO emission in individual clouds, and low recovered emission values reflecting low surface brightness.

quotes.quote: Clouds in NGC 5068 have a lower mean virial parameter than other galaxies whereas clouds in NGC 4826 and NGC 2903 have a higher mean virial parameter.
In contrast, the emission from NGC 5068 appears to be isolated into individual clouds.
The low values in NGC 5068, and to a lesser extent NGC 0628, reflect the low overall surface brightness in these galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5068 as a barred low-mass extreme whose bar still shows a clear age gradient, similar to high-mass NGC 1365, implying the similarity is not mass-driven.

quotes.quote: Out of the 14 barred galaxies in our sample, the only noticeable exceptions are NGC 1365 and NGC 5068, with a very clear age gradient present also in the bar. It is interesting to note that these two galaxies represent the high- and low-stellar mass extremes of our sample ( M _ * /M_ = 10.99 and 9.40, respectively). Therefore, this similarity is likely not connected to their total stellar mass.

gmw_00002378.name: Source 800
gmw_00002378.overview: Source 800 is a star-forming BzK galaxy at a redshift of z=1.784 [arXiv:1312.6364] that is classified as an ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. It is associated with a Chandra X-ray source and may contain an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: ID.03 is the brightest secure CO line source, with follow-up lines fixing z=1.784; it is an infrared-bright BzK galaxy with substantial stellar mass and star formation.

quotes.quote: ID.03 ( =82.80 0.02 GHz) is the brightest line detected in our 3 mm scan. Our PdBI follow-up observations reveal a second line at 165.5 GHz (Fig. fig_al1 ). A third, tentative line is reported at 41.4 GHz in our JVLA observations, albeit only at a SNR of 3.6 .

ID.03 is coincident with an optically faint but infrared bright galaxy. Its AB magnitudes are: B=26.4 , z=24.2 , K=21.9 and thus BzK=(z-K)-(B-z)=0.2 i.e. BzK>-0.2 , the definition of a BzK galaxy following daddi04 . With (B-z)_ AB =2.1 and (z-K)_ AB = 2.3 this galaxy is also placed inside the box of `star forming galaxies at z>1.4 ' Fig. 3 in daddi04 . The galaxy is also detected by Herschel, sampling most of the dust continuum emission elbaz11 .

Based on the SED fitting we derive the following parameters of the galaxy (median-likelihood estimates; 1-- confidence ranges in brackets): SFR= 38_ 1 ^ +8 yr ^ 1 ; M_*=(2.5_ 0.3 ^ +0.1 ) 10^ 11 ; L_ dust = (6.8_ 0.16 ^ +0.65 ) 10^ 11 L _ ; M _ dust = (4.6_ 3.0 ^ +10 ) 10^ 7 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 800 as a retained ULIRG, possibly with an AGN component, and notes a Chandra X-ray source plus brighter near-infrared magnitudes than in the earlier TWS analysis.

quotes.quote: They are sources 439 and 800 in the present catalog. Source 800 retains it ULIRG status in this analysis. Source 800 may also contain an AGN component. Although not listed in their detection catalog, the Chandra X-Ray map of bra01b clearly shows a source at the position of source 800.

The source is the ULIRG, source 800, discussed in sss-ulirg . It corresponds to source 277.211 in TWS where its flux was predicted to be 1.5 mJy rather the 3.46 mJy found here. The present analysis found the same redshift, E(B-V) and template as TWS, but the observed F110W and F160W magnitudes were brighter. If the ULIRG contains a AGN component, as is indicated by the x-ray component, the variation might be real but is of surprising magnitude.

sources.summary: The paper treats ID.03 as a CO-selected source with possible lower-redshift overdensities, low gas mass, and uncertain status as a true bright SMG.

quotes.quote: We note that two SMGs, namely, ID.03 and ID.19, have not been selected through submillimeter surveys but they were found directly through a CO blind survey Decarli14 . Overdensities are also found around the remaining SMG ID.03 with both the B19 and L18 catalogs, but at lower redshifts than that of the SMG, and so the association with the SMG is uncertain. Therefore, unless specified otherwise, in the following we do not consider these overdensities around ID.03.

Interestingly, the source ID.03 is the only SMG in our sample that is not associated with an overdensity in at least one of the three photometric redshift catalogs. These relatively low H _2 gas masses imply that the two sources may not be true SMGs, that is, with strong dust-continuum emission and high molecular gas content. Indeed, ID.19 has been detected in CO(3 2) only Decarli14 , while ID.03 has relatively low-J detections by Decarli14 and Boogaard2023 in CO(1 0) (tentative), CO(2 1), and CO(4 3).

gmw_00002379.name: Source 439
gmw_00002379.overview: Source 439 is classified as a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. It has a revised infrared luminosity of 1.95 times 10 to the 11th power solar luminosities [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: The paper says Source 439, previously source 166 in TWS, drops from ULIRG to LIRG status because the revised template, extinction, and luminosity differ from TWS.

quotes.quote: They are sources 439 and 800 in the present catalog.

Source 439 (166 in TWS), however, falls to LIRG status with a luminosity of 1.95 10^ 11 L _ . The primary reason is that the best fit in this analysis is a template 5.7 and E(B-V) = 0.5 fit as compared to a template 5.9 and E(B-V) = 1.0 fit in TWS. Although the differences in predicted fluxes is small, the luminosity difference between the two fits is large. Given the higher signal to noise, the parameters found in TWS are more likely correct.

gmw_00002380.name: Source 1277
gmw_00002380.overview: Source 1277 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z = 4.8 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. It is a strong source in the F110W and F160W bands, with a luminosity of 1.49e11 solar luminosities and an extinction of E(B-V) = 0.3 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: The paper considers Source 1277 as an alternative HDF850.1 counterpart: it lies closer to the SCUBA position and is a high-redshift, very late object, but its predicted 850 flux is too low.

quotes.quote: An alternative source is 1277 which lies closer than 1 to the position of HDF850.1 given by hug98 . It is barely visible in the F814W band but is a strong source in the F110W and F160W bands. It is a high redshift object (z = 4.8) with a extinction of E(B-V) = 0.3 and a very late template of 5.9. Source 1277 has a luminosity of 1.49 10^ 11 L _ and a predicted 850 flux of 0.125 mJy which is also below the observed flux. The location of the VLA source however makes this identification much less likely. dun02 have proposed an identification of HDF850.1 as a source located between 3-586.0 and 3-593.1 that is only visible after subtraction of 3-586 in ground based K band images.

gmw_00002381.name: HDF850.4
gmw_00002381.overview: HDF850.4 is an infrared galaxy that lies very close to and is blended with the neighboring source HDF850.5 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. These two sources are separated by approximately 12 arcseconds and require deconvolution to be distinguished [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: The paper says HDF850.4 is blended with HDF850.5; source 1108 lies between them, while sources 896 and 905 could identify HDF850.4 with combined predicted flux near the observed value.

quotes.quote: HDF850.4 and HDF850.5 lie very close to each other with a separation of about 12 . The sources are in fact blended and must be deconvolved ser02 . The only nearby source with strong calculated 850 flux is source 1108 which lies almost midway between the sources. Its calculated 850 flux is 1.36 mJy close to the observed fluxes of 2.3 mJy and 2.1 mJy for HDF850.4 and HDF850.5. The source has a spectroscopic redshift of 0.19, an E(B-V) of 0.6 and a luminosity of 1.55 10^ 11 L _ .

If the shift in position for HDF850.1 to lie on top of 3-593 is applied to HDF850.4 and HDF850.5, HDF850.5 would be within 4 of 1108. In this case sources 896 and 905 could be identified with HDF850.4. Their combined predicted flux is equal to 0.89 mJy which is near to the observed flux of 2.3 mJy for HDF850.4.

gmw_00002382.name: HDF850.5
gmw_00002382.overview: HDF850.5 is an infrared galaxy that lies very close to the neighboring source HDF850.4 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. These two sources are blended and require deconvolution, with the nearby source 1108 situated almost midway between them [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: The paper describes HDF850.5 as blended with HDF850.4, with source 1108 lying between the pair and potentially within four arcseconds of HDF850.5 after applying the HDF850.1 positional shift.

quotes.quote: HDF850.4 and HDF850.5 lie very close to each other with a separation of about 12 . The sources are in fact blended and must be deconvolved ser02 . The only nearby source with strong calculated 850 flux is source 1108 which lies almost midway between the sources. Its calculated 850 flux is 1.36 mJy close to the observed fluxes of 2.3 mJy and 2.1 mJy for HDF850.4 and HDF850.5. The source has a spectroscopic redshift of 0.19, an E(B-V) of 0.6 and a luminosity of 1.55 10^ 11 L _ .
If the shift in position for HDF850.1 to lie on top of 3-593 is applied to HDF850.4 and HDF850.5, HDF850.5 would be within 4 of 1108.

gmw_00002383.name: source 1108
gmw_00002383.overview: Source 1108 is an infrared galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of 0.19 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. It has a luminosity of 1.55 × 10^11 solar luminosities, an extinction E(B-V) of 0.6, and a calculated 850 micron flux of 1.36 mJy [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 1108 as the strongest nearby calculated 850 micron source between HDF850.4 and HDF850.5, with redshift, extinction, luminosity, and flux close to the observed pair.

quotes.quote: HDF850.4 and HDF850.5 lie very close to each other with a separation of about 12 . The sources are in fact blended and must be deconvolved ser02 . The only nearby source with strong calculated 850 flux is source 1108 which lies almost midway between the sources. Its calculated 850 flux is 1.36 mJy close to the observed fluxes of 2.3 mJy and 2.1 mJy for HDF850.4 and HDF850.5. The source has a spectroscopic redshift of 0.19, an E(B-V) of 0.6 and a luminosity of 1.55 10^ 11 L _ .

gmw_00002384.name: VLA 3651+1221
gmw_00002384.overview: VLA 3651+1221 is a very red radio and X-ray source located near the elliptical galaxy 3-659.1 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594]. Due to its X-ray emission, the object is likely an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0306594].

sources.summary: The paper describes VLA 3651+1221 as a very red VLA and X-ray source near elliptical galaxy 3-659.1, arguing its catalog identification mixes flux from two different galaxies.

quotes.quote: VLA 3651+1221 dwn99 observed a second source, VLA 3651+1221, very near the elliptical galaxy 3-659.1 which is source 1193 in Table tab1 . The F160W image shown in Figure fig-vla clearly shows a strong source at the position of the VLA detection that is very faint or absent in the F814W image. It is clearly a very red source. The correspondence was first noticed by dicet00 who claim that this is the second reddest source in the NHDF. pap01 point out that the object is also an x-ray source hor00, bra01a and therefore probably an AGN. The SE source extraction program linked the source with the elliptical galaxy in producing Table tab1 , which resulted in an identification of a very late galaxy with a high extinction.
This is clearly an artifact of the superposition of fluxes from two very different types of galaxies.

gmw_00002385.name: SXDF-NB209-17
gmw_00002385.overview: SXDF-NB209-17 is a massive, compact star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.182 [arXiv:1311.4260]. It exhibits a relaxed, bulge-like morphology and contains an old stellar population, suggesting it is likely in a post-starburst evolutionary phase [arXiv:1311.4260].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SXDF-NB209-17 as a massive compact star-forming galaxy at z=2.182 with red-nugget-like density, bulge-like morphology, old stellar population, and likely post-starburst character.

quotes.quote: In our emitter sample, we find two massive, compact star-forming galaxies with M _*>10^ 11 M _ and r_ e <2 kpc (SXDF-NB209-17 and SXDF-NB2315-7 in Figure fig;M_Re ). Their surface stellar mass densities are as high as those of red nuggets at z=1.5 -2.0 2012ApJ...746..162N . Moreover, the two compact HAEs show high S e rsic indices of n 2 , which indicate relaxed bulge-like morphologies. They can directly turn into compact quiescent galaxies at high-redshift just by stopping their star-formation activities without changing the sizes or structures. Note that SXDF-NB209-17 has been spectroscopically confirmed to be located at z = 2.182 (Tadaki et al., in preparation).
