The absence of large-scale jets and the pronounced curvature of the integrated radio spectrum of the entire source in the range 150-4850 MHz (spectral curvature, SPC=1.5; murgia2011 ) suggest that the lobes represent remnants of a past phase of jet activity.

The remarkable spatial coincidence observed between the newly detected arc-like radio filament and the southern concave X-ray discontinuity strongly suggests that the remnant plasma has been displaced by the sloshing motions on large scales. Overall, NGC 507 represents one of the clearest examples known to date in which a direct interaction between old AGN remnant plasma and the external medium is observed in a galaxy group.

sources.summary: The MASSIVE survey paper identifies NGC 507 as a slow rotator with a kinematically distinct component, disk-like inner rotation, outer rotation, and a mild kinematic twist.

quotes.quote: We find kinematically distinct components (KDC) in NGC 507 and NGC 5322. Both are slow rotators with k_1^ max 50 km s ^ 1 and _e below 0.1. The KDC of NGC 507 was reported in our earlier paper Vealeetal2017a .

The velocity map and kinematic parameters for NGC 507 are shown in Fig. fig:0507 . The local kinematic position angle (R) shows a large jump of 100 between a radius of 10'' and 20'' . The inner kinematic component extends to R 10'' and exhibits a disk-like rotation with k_1 50 km s ^ 1 around a median PA _ kin of 185 . The outer component rotates at 40 km s ^ 1 around a median PA _ kin of 290 , and it shows a mild twist of 25 around this outer axis.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 0507 as a central massive elliptical and brightest group member whose cool-core X-ray brightness contrast exceeds unity, with group-scale thermodynamic and abundance behavior.

quotes.quote: Except for NGC 0507, MKW4, and A1644, the observed values of | I_ X |/ I_ X are smaller than unity, and the distributions for the HIFLUGCS and CLASH samples are fairly similar. We note that two of the outliers having | I_ X |/ I_ X > 1 are less massive, galaxy--group scale objects. NGC 0507 ( z=0.01646 ) is the brightest member galaxy of a nearby galaxy group.

To investigate this possibility, we focus on three outliers having | I_ X | / I_ X > 1 , namely, NGC 0507, MKW4, and A1644. For NGC 0507 and MKW4 with kT_ pos, neg <2 keV, the metal abundance in the positive excess regions is comparable to that in the negative excess regions (see Table tab:fit ).

We found three outliers, namely NGC 0507, MKW4, and A1644, whose brightness contrast exceeds unity. NGC 0507 and MKW4 are low mass, galaxy--group scale objects, whereas A1644 is a post merger exhibiting a clear spiral-like X-ray morphology in the central region.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC507 as a nearby group-central galaxy with a weak B2 radio source, Chandra X-ray edges, displaced emitting gas, and unclear sharp discontinuities.

quotes.quote: NGC507 is the central galaxy in a nearby ( z=0.016 ) group that has been studied extensively in X-rays . The galaxy is the site of a weak B2 radio source (luminosity 10^ 37 ergs s ^ 1 ) . The Chandra X-ray image, shown in Fig. wrfn507 a, covers only the central, high surface brightness emission of the group around NGC507. The 0.5-2.0 keV surface brightness distribution shows sharp edges to the southwest, southeast and north, reminiscent of those in the clusters A2142 and A3667.

Thus, it seems likely that the radio lobe, probably a buoyant bubble, has displaced X-ray emitting gas generating a trough in the X-ray surface brightness distribution. The origin of the peculiar sharp surface brightness discontinuities around NGC507 is unclear.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 507 as a dominant elliptical with FR I source B2 0120+33, distorted radio lobes, and disturbed asymmetric X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 507 (Arp 229) is the dominant elliptical galaxy in the richest group of galaxies (Mulchaey et al. 2003; O'Sullivan et al. 2003; Jeltema et al. 2008) in the Pisces system of groups. It hosts the low-power and extended FR I radio source B2 0120+33 (Parma et al. 1986). The GMRT full-resolution image at 610 MHz is presented in Fig. fig:ngc507 (left) as contours on the Chandra image. In the right panel, we show the overlay of the 235 MHz contours on the optical image. The radio source is very distorted and asymmetric.

sources.summary: The paper identifies B2 0120+33 with NGC 507, describing dense-cluster X-ray emission, a weak-core steep radio spectrum, asymmetric HRI structure, and unresolved-emission limits.

quotes.quote: B2 0120+33 has a steep radio spectrum and weak core. It may be a source with particularly weak jets, or possibly a remnant of a radio galaxy whose nuclear engine is almost inactive and whose luminosity has decreased due to synchrotron or adiabatic losses (Fanti et al. 1987). The HRI map shows the central region of this source to be asymmetrical, with a large extended emission region to the SW. Radial-profile fitting of the innermost parts of this galaxy with point, , and point+ models, show that a good fit to the data is achieved by using a single model with =0.67, which gives a core radius of 4 .

sources.summary: NGC 507 is treated as the brightest Pisces Cluster group member, with extended east-west radio lobes, a bright X-ray halo, and central disturbance around its radio structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 507 is the brightest member of a group of galaxies that is part of the Pisces Cluster. Our radio map has comparatively poor spatial resolution but show that the source is highly extended with two bright lobes in a roughly east-west configuration. ROSAT X-ray observations showed NGC 507 to have an extended, bright X-ray halo with a luminosity comparable to that of poor clusters Kriss83, Kim95 . The Chandra data show some disturbance at the centre of the cluster with indications of a shell of enhanced emission around the eastern radio lobe Kraft04 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies B2 0120+33 as the low-power FR I radio galaxy hosted by NGC 507, with asymmetric lobes interpreted as remnants of past jet activity.

quotes.quote: The galaxy NGC 507 hosts a low-power ( P_ 150 MHz =1 10^ 24 W Hz^ 1 ) Fanaroff-Riley I fanaroff1974 radio galaxy named B2 0120+33 parma1986, giacintucci2011, murgia2011 . The radio source shows two asymmetric lobes with a total extension of 70 kpc (see Fig. fig:rgb ) and a faint, unresolved central component that has been detected at frequencies 610 MHz with power equal to P_ core,1400 MHz =1.3 10^ 21 W Hz^ 1 giacintucci2011, murgia2011 . The absence of large-scale jets and the pronounced curvature of the integrated radio spectrum of the entire source in the range 150-4850 MHz (spectral curvature, SPC=1.5; murgia2011 ) suggest that the lobes represent remnants of a past phase of jet activity. In particular, using spectral ageing models komissarov1994 , estimated the total age of the plasma to be in the range 70 140 Myr and that the jets switched off 50 Myr ago.

sources.summary: NGC507 is presented as a central group galaxy with a weak radio source and Chandra X-ray surface-brightness edges around its group-center emission.

quotes.quote: NGC507 is the central galaxy in a nearby ( z=0.016 ) group that has been studied extensively in X-rays . The galaxy is the site of a weak B2 radio source (luminosity 10^ 37 ergs s ^ 1 ) . The Chandra X-ray image, shown in Fig. n507 a, covers only the central, high surface brightness emission of the group around NGC507. The 0.5-2.0 keV surface brightness distribution shows sharp edges to the southwest, southeast and north, reminiscent of those in the clusters A2142 and A3667.

sources.summary: The review reports abundance and radio-lobe features in NGC507, interpreting its abundance front as transport from the galaxy center or as sloshing/merger-related structure.

quotes.quote: A similar case may be that of NGC507; again, no 2D metal abundance map is available, but Kraft:2004 report a gradient in the elemental abundance across a sharp arc-like X-ray surface brightness discontinuity with opening angle of 125 deg. Because that discontinuity is aligned with a low surface brightness radio lobe, the authors conclude that this `abundance front' can be explained by the transport of high-abundance material from the center of the galaxy due to the transonic inflation of the radio lobe; however, it was subsequently realized (e.g. see previous paragraph) that classical cold fronts are likely to produce such abundance arcs as well. The abundance feature in NGC507 is therefore not unusual and could be simply due to classical sloshing, or to an interaction between AGN feedback and past merging activity.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 507 as a Pisces-cluster CDG in a merging group system, with large-scale diffuse FR I radio lobes and related X-ray cavities.

quotes.quote: NGC 507* NGC 507 is the CDG of the NGC 507 group, which belongs to the Pisces cluster. As mentioned in the previous section, the NGC 507 group is likely to be merging with the NGC 499 group (Section sec:ngc499 ). \ The large-scale diffuse radio emission from a low-power FR I radio source B2 0120+33 parma1986 has a shape resembling a letter ` ' formed by two radio lobes with diameters of 40 and 20 kpc dunn2010, murgia2011 .

gmw_00002481.overview: 7572 is an edge-on quiescent lenticular disk galaxy that substantially exceeds the Milky Way in size and mass [arXiv:1912.04887]. Its structure contains flaring thin and thick stellar disks, with the old thick disk being unusually massive compared to the younger thin component [arXiv:1912.04887].

sources.summary: The paper studies 7572 as an enormous edge-on quiescent S0 disk galaxy with flaring thin and thick disks and an unusually massive old thick disk.

quotes.quote: Here we probe the thick disc formation scenarios by investigating 7572, an enormous edge-on galaxy having R_ 25 25 kpc and V_ rot 370 , which substantially exceeds the Milky Way size and mass. We analysed DECaLS archival imaging and found that the disc of 7572 contains two flaring stellar discs (a thin and a thick disc) with similar radial scales. We collected deep long-slit spectroscopic data using the 6-m Russian BTA telescope and analysed them with a novel technique. We first reconstructed a non-parametric stellar line-of-sight velocity distribution along the radius of the galaxy and then fitted it with two kinematic components accounting for the orbital distribution of stars in thin and thick discs.

The old thick disc turned out to be 2.4 times as massive as the intermediate-age thin component, 1.9 10^ 11 vs. 7.8 10^ 10 , which is very unusual. The different duration of the formation epochs evidenced by the [ Mg/Fe ] values of +0.3 dex and +0.15 dex for the thick and thin discs respectively, their kinematics and the mass ratio suggest that in 7572 we observe a rapidly formed very massive thick disc and an under-developed thin disc, whose formation ended prematurely due to the exhaustion of the cold gas likely because of environmental effects.

gmw_00002482.name: J2346+0705
gmw_00002482.overview: J2346+0705 is a radio-loud quasar or host galaxy associated with the radio source TXS 2344+068 [arXiv:2007.09038, arXiv:1711.05476]. Although previously reported at a higher redshift, its optical spectrum shows weak absorption lines that favor a low-redshift classification of z = 0.171 [arXiv:2007.09038, arXiv:1711.05476].

sources.summary: The paper uses VLBI jet proper motions to argue J2346+0705 is not a z>5 quasar but a low-redshift radio-loud quasar or host galaxy.

quotes.quote: As an example, we present the case of the quasar J2346+0705 that was reported with two different redshifts, z_ 1 = 5.063 and z_ 2 = 0.171 , in the literature. We measured the apparent proper motions ( ) of three components identified in its radio jet by utilizing VLBI data taken from 2014 to 2018. We obtained _ J1 = 0.334 0.099 mas yr ^ 1 , _ J2 = 0.116 0.029 mas yr ^ 1 , and _ J3 = 0.060 0.005 mas yr ^ 1 .

The maximal proper motion is converted to an apparent transverse speed of _ app = 41.2 12.2 . if the source is at redshift 5.063. This value exceeds the blazar jet speeds known to date. This and other arguments suggest that J2346+0705 is hosted by a low-redshift galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FGLJ2346.7+0705 with TXS 2344+068 and favors z = 0.171 from weak Ca II, G-band, and Mg I absorption superimposed on a nonthermal continuum.

quotes.quote: 3FGLJ2346.7+0705 In the 3LAC catalog, this source is associated to the radio source TXS 2344+068 and classified as BCU-II. There is a quasi-featureless optical spectrum provided by SDSS survey that, depending on the Data Release, proposed two different redshift values (z = 0.171 and z = 5.06). In our spectrum we detect weak absorptions of Ca II (3934 , 3968 ), G-band (4305 ) and Mg I (5157 ), corresponding to a redshift of 0.171, superimposed to the non-thermal continuum.

gmw_00002483.name: Galaxy-D
gmw_00002483.overview: Galaxy-D is a compact, massive quiescent galaxy [arXiv:2107.13241]. Its primary episode of star formation had already elapsed by z~6 to z~8, after which the galaxy experienced a rapid decline in its star formation rate [arXiv:2107.13241].

sources.summary: Galaxy-D is described as a compact massive quiescent galaxy in RO-1001, with GALFIT morphology and optical/IR constraints indicating early star formation and sustained quenching.

quotes.quote: In order to ensure that all the flux from Galaxy-D is accounted for, we use galaxy morphological model fitting with the software GALFIT. Since the 1.6 m (F160W) image has the highest S/N, we use it for this attempt. We fit s ersic profiles to both Galaxy-D and the foreground galaxy, along with Gaussian profiles to multiple surrounding objects (Fig. fig:galfit ). We measure a total AB-magnitude of 24.00 for Galaxy-D and get a s ersic index of 2.0 0.2 .

Hence, it can be unambiguously concluded that Galaxy-D is truly quiescent, consistently from both the optical and IR side. Based on the mass-weighted ( t_ 50 ) as well as light-weighted age limits (Sec. sec:galaxy_param ), the primary episode of star-formation had already elapsed by z 6 and most likely by z 8 (Fig. fig:age_chi_sfh , left), right after which it likely experienced a rapid decline in its SFR characterised by the best-fit delayed model with =0.2 Gyr (Fig. fig:age_chi_sfh , right).

gmw_00002484.name: Leo IV
gmw_00002484.overview: Leo IV is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1709.04867, arXiv:1901.05465] located approximately 151 to 160 kpc away [arXiv:1802.01581, arXiv:astro-ph/0608448]. It is dominated by an ancient, metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:1310.0824] that completed most of its star formation before the end of reionization [arXiv:1901.05465].

sources.summary: The paper treats Leo IV as one of two Milky Way dwarf galaxies whose apparent stellar bridge is reanalyzed; deeper Subaru data yield structural parameters and suggest the overdensity overlaps rather than connects the satellites.

quotes.quote: Box 9513, 2300 RA Leiden, The Netherlands ^5 Institute of Astronomy, University of Cambridge, Madingley Road, Cambridge CB3 0HA, UK We present a revised analysis of a speculated stellar bridge between the Milky Way dwarf galaxies Leo IV and Leo V. Using data acquired with Subaru/Suprime-Cam over a 1 ^ 4 ^ field encompassing the two satellites and the region in between, we confirm our previous detection of a stellar overdensity between Leo IV and Leo V 2010ApJ...710.1664D . The larger area coverage and improved depth of our current dataset allow for an improved analysis of the stellar overdensity that had previously appeared to bridge the two galaxies.

The constellation that boasts the largest number of Milky Way satellites is the realm of Leo, and two of its five dwarf-galaxy inhabitants form the subject of our current study. Discovered by , Leo IV and Leo V are close neighbours not just in projection --- separated on the sky by a mere 3 ^ -- but also spatially, sharing residence of the Galactic halo beyond 150 kpc. Targeted observations for studying these Milky Way satellites and their surrounding environment by using the LAICA imager on the 3.5m telescope at the Calar Alto observatory led to the unexpected detection of an overdensity of stars, seemingly bridging the two galaxies. However, the loss of one of three planned fields due to bad weather unfortunately left us with only a tentative detection of the possible stellar bridge, whose true nature unfortunately could not be resolved from these data alone. Follow-up observations using Subaru/Suprime-Cam aimed at obtaining a wider and deeper dataset has enabled us to shed clearer light on the nature of this stellar overdensity, as well as allowing for an improved set of structural parameters to be derived for Leo IV and Leo V.

sources.summary: The paper uses Leo IV as a representative UFDG: MCMC gives preferred PBH-DM parameters and a broad PBH mass range, but the likeliest simulation overpredicts its half-mass radius.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:monochromatic-2-15-Leo-IV we show the corner plot of the posterior distributions for R_ 0, DM , R_ 0, , M_ DM , and M_ resulting from the MCMC analysis on the stellar velocity dispersion and half-light radius of Leo IV (cf. Zhu_et_al_2018 , figure 5). There, we use a stellar mass-to-light ratio of 2 M _ / L _ and =15 . The posteriors for quantities which followed a log-uniform prior ( R_ 0, DM , R_ 0, , and M_ ) quickly develop a pronounced peak, whereas the posterior for M_ DM is dominated by our choice of a log-normal prior.
For Leo IV, a wide range, M_ [ 8,162 ] M _ , is thus allowed.

For instance, running a simulation (stellar mass-to-light ratio =2 M _ / L _ , =15 ) of Leo IV with its likeliest input parameters, R_ 0, DM =10^ 3.16 pc , R_ 0, =10^ 2.03 pc , M_ DM =10^ 9.03 , and M_ =10^ 1.84 , i.e. the maxima of the posterior distributions in Figure fig:monochromatic-2-15-Leo-IV (cf. Table tab:preferences-2-15 ), yields the stellar velocity dispersion _ ,S =3.6 km s ^ 1 and the deprojected half-mass radius R_ h, ,S =181.4 pc. As done in Section subsec:observation , we explicitly retain the subscript S in order to indicate values as a result of a simulation -- in contrast to the use of D for the observational data. This result has to be compared with the observed values _ ,D =3.3 1.7 km s ^ 1 and R_ h, ,D =(4/3)r_ h, ,D =152 16 pc (cf. Table tab:sample ). While the simulated and observed stellar velocity dispersions agree well with each other, the proposed half-mass radius is actually too large to meet the observed one within the given measurement uncertainty.

sources.summary: The paper treats Leo IV as a counterexample to a common dwarf-spheroidal mass scale, finding its interior mass at 300 pc is at most 3 million solar masses.

quotes.quote: Fig. fig:comparis shows that even within the uncertainties, Hercules and Leo IV are not compatible with a common mass scale of 10^7 .

Another counter-example is provided by Leo IV. Here, 300 pc corresponds to 2.6 , and Fig. fig:comparis shows that the interior mass is again at most 3 10^6 . We conclude that the common mass-scale of St08 is illusory.

However, there are some objects with intermediate half-light radii, like Hercules and Leo IV for which the common mass-scale clearly fails. It is worth noting that the agreement betwen our masses and earlier investigators is often good. Where results do differ -- as for example in Hercules or Leo IV -- it is often because velocity disperion measurements have been revised.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a compact distant RR Lyrae group with Leo IV, detects two known RRab stars, notes a missed third, and revises its distance.

quotes.quote: Among the sample of distant RRLs, we found two distant groups of closely spaced RRL both in angular separation and heliocentric distances. In these cases, they coincide with the position on the sky and distance of the ultra-faint galaxies Leo IV and Leo V.

Out of the three known RRLs in Leo IV Moretti2009 , we detected two stars, HiTS113256-003329 and HiTS113259-003404, both RRab. The non-detection of the third RRLs (called V3 by Moretti2009 ) is consistent with the detection efficiency expected for RRLs of these magnitudes, according to our results (Section 3).
Using [ Fe/H ] =-2.31 for Leo IV Simon2007 , we get a mean heliocentric distance of 151.4 4.4 kpc. This number is in agreement with the estimation made by Moretti2009 ( 154 5 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper presents new HST proper motions for Leo IV and finds its phase-space and orbital properties broadly consistent with the proposed Crater-Leo group infall scenario.

quotes.quote: The proposed Crater-Leo group, represented in Figure fig:positions , consists of Leo II, a classical dwarf spheroidal galaxy, Leo IV and Leo V, both ultra-faint satellite galaxies, Crater 1, a globular cluster, and Crater II, a distant diffuse satellite galaxy. Properties Leo IV and Leo V were first proposed as a pair to explain both their spatial proximity ( <3 ) and small differences in their line-of-sight distances ( <20 kpc) and velocities ( <40 kms ^ 1 ) jong_enigmatic_2010 . Their closeness is especially unusual since the outer halo of the MW is mostly empty, with only 20 of all currently known dwarfs inside a 400 kpc radius located beyond 150 kpc from the Galactic centre Torrealba2016 . Following the discoveries of Crater 1 belokurov_atlas_2014 and Crater II Torrealba2016 , the possible association between these objects was further explored. Together with Leo II, these objects align along a common great circle (see left panel of Figure fig:positions ), which is highly unlikely. Torrealba2016 estimated the probability of this happening randomly - without sharing a common group origin - using random Monte Carlo realisations of satellite distributions on the sky and determining the largest group of satellites that are strongly aligned for each realisation. They find that having apparent aligned groups with five or more members by random chance has a probability of only 0.0045 . In addition, the objects show a monotonic distance gradient (see middle panel of Figure fig:positions ), moving up in declination from Crater II ( D_h = 116.6 6.6 kpc) to Leo II ( D_h = 216.8 11.0 kpc). To make this argument even more compelling, the line-of-sight velocities of these objects seem to prefer a common value of about zero in the Galactic rest frame The root mean square (RMS) scatter is 40 km/s, which can be compared to the larger one-dimensional velocity dispersion of 100 km/s for the MW satellite population as a whole riley_velocity_2019 . When we exclude Crater II, this RMS scatter becomes 25 km/s. (see right panel of Figure fig:positions ), with Crater II being the most offset in velocity. Furthermore, fattahi_galaxy_2013 show that satellite galaxies close in position and velocity preferentially form pairs of comparable luminosity. When we look at the luminosity of these objects, Crater 1, Leo IV, and Leo V have comparable luminosities ( M_ V = -5.3 , M_ V = -5.8 and M_ V = -5.2 , respectively), as well as Leo II and Crater II ( M_ V = -9.8 and M_ V = -8.2 , respectively). These values are described in Table tab:properties . ht References: (1) munoz_megacam_2018 ; (2) irwin_structural_1995 ; (3) gullieuszik_evolved_2008 ; (4) spencer_binary_2017 ; (5) kirby_universal_2013 ; (6) McConnachie2012 ; (7) jong_enigmatic_2010 ; (8) moretti_leo_2009 ; (9) jenkins_very_2021 ; (10) mutlu-pakdil_signatures_2019 ; (11) belokurov_atlas_2014 ; (12) weisz_hubble_2016 ; (13) kirby_spectroscopic_2015 ; (14) vivas_decam_2020 ; (15) Torrealba2016 ; (16) caldwell_crater_2017 ; (17) fu_dynamical_2019 . ^* For the dwarf galaxies (Leo II, Leo IV, Leo V and Crater II), M corresponds to the dynamical mass inside the half-light radius. For the globular cluster (Crater 1), due to its lack of dark matter, M corresponds to its stellar mass. Finally, some of these objects also seem to share similar star formation histories (SFHs). The SFHs of Leo II and Leo IV indicate that these objects ended their star formation around 5-6 Gyr ago weisz_star_2014 .

We present new measurements for the proper motions of Leo IV and Leo V dwarf galaxies using multiepoch HST imaging data. Our final results for the PMs are ( _ , _ ) = (-0.1921 0.0514, -0.0686 0.0523) mas yr ^ 1 for Leo IV and ( _ , _ ) = (0.1186 0.1943, -0.1183 0.1704) mas yr ^ 1 for Leo V. We start by determining the orbital poles of the putative group with the proper motions available from Gaia DR3, and the new HST proper motions presented in this paper. The possible orbital poles suggest that these objects can share a common value for their specific angular momentum within their uncertainties. When we take into account the new HST proper motion measurements, a common orbital pole becomes even more likely. We use the method proposed by to predict the proper motions of the considered satellites, assuming that they share a common group origin. Our proper motion predictions are consistent with the measured proper motions within the errors for most of the proposed members. Based on Gaia DR3 measurements alone, the predictions of the proper motions for Leo IV and Leo V seem to prefer the positive circulation about the northern pole. In contrast, Leo II and Crater 1 predictions clearly favour the negative circulation. However, a common group accretion requires a common orbiting direction. When we consider our new HST measurements, this changes and all four objects prefer the one co-orbiting direction ( h- ). Better proper motion measurements with lower uncertainties are required to confirm this association with higher confidence. Crater II, however, has its proper motion well constrained and does not share a similar orbital pole with the rest of the objects, nor does it match our proper motion predictions. Furthermore, Crater II is the object with the highest offset from the common value of the line-of-sight shared by the group. We can thus rule out an association with the other members. Other objects were identified as possible additional members of the Crater-Leo group due to their orbital poles. Yet, their proper motion predictions are far from the measured ones, suggesting that they were not part of it. Based on HST and Gaia DR3 proper motions, we have performed orbital analyses for the four satellites that we consider as possibly associated, Leo II, Leo IV, Leo V and Crater 1, to try to understand if they have a shared orbital history or if there is any evidence that the objects have been bound in the past. For both considered potentials, all objects are near their apocentre at the present time. All objects are consistent with having reached their pericentre around the same time, within their uncertainties. When we look at the separation of Leo IV, Leo V and Crater 1 with respect to Leo II, they are all at comparable distances from each other 3 Gyr ago, suggesting that there is a physical association between these objects and not just a chance alignment at the present time. The derived orbital properties are consistent, within their observational uncertainties, with those we predict from assuming group infall, especially when we compare with the properties obtained with our new HST proper motions. We show that the bound orbits derived from the observational uncertainties match the orbits derived from the predicted PMs. These are the orbits that not only have PMs consistent with observations, but that also have similar specific energy and angular momentum. Our assumptions can thus explain why these align along a great circle at the present time. When we consider the effect that the infall of the LMC might have had on the orbital properties of our putative group, it is found that the probability of the objects sharing the same specific angular momentum and specific energy increases. Once again, it seems that Crater II was not accreted with the other satellites. When we shift our predicted proper motions back to the centre of mass of the current MW halo, to compare with the current measurements, we get similar results to the previous ones, suggesting that the infall of the LMC did not have a strong effect on the orbital poles of the considered objects, not surprisingly, as they largely reside on the opposite side of the MW. The predictions that the proper motions seem to prefer the negative circulation about the northern pole are strengthened when we consider the new proper motion measurements. Leo II, Leo IV, Leo V, and Crater 1 show orbital properties consistent with those we predict from assuming group infall; however, uncertainties of the current proper motion measurements with both Gaia and HST are too high to conclusively confirm their dynamical associations.

sources.summary: Leo IV is a distant HST ultra-faint dwarf target; the paper links it to synchronized ancient stellar populations and notes its IMF is flatter than those in the Galactic field.

quotes.quote: For the distant ( > 130 kpc) satellites in the sample (Hercules, Leo IV, and Canes Venatici II), we surveyed a narrow pencil beam (1 or 2 tiles in each camera) more deeply (10--16 orbits, faint limit of V 29 mag). The varying depth and area of each survey was intended to provide a sample of a few hundred stars in the vicinity of the MSTO with a photometric precision of 0.01 mag, thus providing tight constraints on age when coupled with independent knowledge of the metallicity distribution from Keck.

It is clear that the CMD of each UFD looks like the CMD of an ancient globular cluster, and that each is dominated by an old (age > 12 Gyr) population of metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] < -2 ) stars. A prominent blue straggler sequence (BSS) is present in each galaxy -- ubiquitous in old populations.

The HST photometry of these six UFD galaxies demonstrates that they are dominated by truly ancient stars ( > 12 Gyr old), and that their SFHs are largely synchronized (with our initial estimates indicating agreement at the level of 1 Gyr).

sources.summary: The paper presents Leo IV as a dwarf galaxy in the ultra-faint sample, citing metallicity measurements, new abundance work, and probable pre-reionization star formation.

quotes.quote: Among these fives galaxies, two (Leo IV and Hercules) are consistent with the hypothesis that the bulk of their stars were formed before reionization weisz2014 . Leo IV The dwarf galaxy Leo IV was discovered by belo2007 along with Coma Berenices, Canes Venatici II and Hercules. obtained Keck/DEIMOS spectra of a sample of stars belonging to this galaxy and derived a metallicity of [ Fe/H ] = 2.31 0.10 dex. The first determination of the chemical composition of stars in Leo IV was done by . They obtained high resolution Magellan/MIKE spectra of the brightest star in Leo IV and measured an iron abundance [ Fe/H ] = 3.2 dex with an element enhancement similar to what is found in the milky way halo.
We present in this paper, the determination of the detailed chemical composition of two stars belonging to LeoIV. vargas2013 revised the metallicity and found [ Fe/H ] = 2.89 0.11 dex .

sources.summary: The paper treats Leo IV as an ultra-faint dwarf satellite in a homogeneous very metal-poor stellar-parameter sample for comparing dwarf-galaxy and Milky Way halo populations.

quotes.quote: We present a homogeneous set of accurate atmospheric parameters for a complete sample of very and extremely metal-poor stars in the dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSphs) Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Sextans, Fornax, Bo otes I, Ursa Major II, and Leo IV.

The comparison between ultra-faint, classical dwarf spheroidal galaxies (UFDs, dSphs), and the Milky Way population offers a fantastic opportunity to probe different galaxy masses, star formation histories, levels of chemical enrichement. Both nucleosynthetic processes and galaxy formation models largely benefit from the diversity of the populations sampled that way.

We selected 36 stars in total in the classical dSphs Sculptor (Scl), Ursa Minor (UMi), Fornax (Fnx), and Sextans (Sex) and the ultra-faint dwarfs Bo otes I, Ursa Major II (UMa II), and Leo IV (Table Tab:spectra ). This sample covers the 4 Fe/H < -1.5 metallicity range.

sources.summary: The paper uses Simon probabilities for Leo IV, retains only three distant faint members, finds supporting photometric stars, and notes the motion poorly constrains system properties.

quotes.quote: Leo IV For this galaxy we use the catalog of Simon_07 , which provides binary probabilities. Due to the galaxy distance and faintness, only 3 members remain after our cuts. A few faint stars w/o spectroscopy have a consistent motion to our measurement. Also the background is low at the large distance of this galaxy. Due to the faintness of the three stars the error of the motion is still rather large and does not constrain much the properties of the system.

sources.summary: The review cites Leo IV as an ultra-faint dwarf with an old stellar population, HST-derived star-formation history, and IMF measurements with large uncertainties.

quotes.quote: Star Formation Histories The first analysis of deep HST imaging of UFDs was carried out by brown12 , studying Hercules, Leo IV, and UMa I. They concluded that the three galaxies have similar ages, and are each as old or older than the prototypical ancient globular cluster M92. brown14 expanded the sample to six UFDs, adding Boo I, Canes Venatici II (CVn II), and Coma Berenices (Com Ber) to the previous three. By incorporating improved spectroscopic MDFs and updated isochrones matched to observed dwarf galaxy chemical abundance patterns, brown14 determined that all of the galaxies except UMa I had formed more than 75 of their stars by z 10 . Using a star formation model consisting of two bursts, the best fit for UMa I has approximately half of its stars forming at z 3 . A large majority of the stars in all six dwarfs had formed by the end of reionization at z 6 , consistent with the idea that gas heating by reionization ended star formation in such objects e.g., bullock00,somerville02,benson02 . Note, however, that quenching by reionization does not necessarily mean that star formation ends precisely at the redshift of reionization, since sufficiently high-density molecular gas can survive somewhat beyond reionization even in low-mass halos e.g., onorbe15 . Star formation histories have also been derived for Hercules, Leo IV, and CVn II by weisz14a from shallower WFPC2 data. weisz14a found that >90 of the stars in Hercules and Leo IV are older than 11 Gyr, consistent with the brown14 results.

geha13 measured the IMF in two ultra-faint dwarfs, Hercules and Leo IV, using star counts from the HST photometry of brown12 . Over the mass range from 0.5-0.8 , they found that the best fitting power law had a slope of 1.2 , much shallower than the salpeter55 value of = 2.35 . While the uncertainties for Leo IV are quite large, in Hercules the slope disagrees with a Salpeter IMF at 5.8 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Leo IV members, resolves its velocity dispersion at 95 percent confidence, measures its metallicity properties, and notes sensitivity to one high-metallicity member star.

quotes.quote: Leo IV We identify 20 member stars in Leo IV, including nine new members. Using this membership catalogue, we calculate a systematic velocity of 131.6 ^ +1.0 _ 1.2 km s ^ 1 , velocity dispersion of 3.4 ^ +1.3 _ 0.9 km s ^ 1 , systematic metallicity of 2.48 ^ +0.16 _ 0.13 dex and metallicity dispersion of 0.42 ^ +0.12 _ 0.10 dex . This is the first time the velocity dispersion of Leo IV has been resolved at the 95 confidence level. We measure one member star with a metallicity of 1.30 0.15 dex. When this star is excluded from metallicity calculations, we are unable to resolve the Leo IV metallicity dispersion.

sources.summary: The paper presents Leo IV as a new probable dwarf galaxy satellite at roughly 160 kpc, with a 160 pc half-light radius and a complex CMD indicating a thick giant branch and blue horizontal branch.

quotes.quote: The first row of Figure  fig:objsa refers to a satellite in Coma Berenices (henceforth Com), the second to a satellite in Canes Venatici (henceforth CVn II), the third to a probable globular cluster (henceforth Segue 1), the fourth to a satellite in Hercules (henceforth Her) and the fifth to a satellite in Leo (henceforth Leo IV)  There is already a dwarf galaxy known in the constellation of Canes Venatici  Zu06a , and two known in Leo. Leo III is an alternative name for the Leo dwarf irregular, also called Leo A  Be00 . .

Leo IV is at a distance of 160^ +15 _ 14 kpc. Its half-light radius is 160 pc. Its CMD is more complex than the others, with an apparent thick giant branch and a blue horizontal branch. The thickness may be caused by multiple stellar populations and/or by depth along the line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper describes Leo IV as a low-mass dwarf with few abundance measurements, uncertain alpha trends, and luminosity implying more star formation than the authors’ models.

quotes.quote: Leo IV (yellow in Figure f:vargasmetals ) has a half-light radius mass of 1.2 ^ +3.5 _ 0.9 10^6 M _ wolf10 and contains only four observed stars, all with large uncertainties in their abundance ratios. More stellar abundances are required to determine whether [ /Fe ] decreases with increasing [ Fe/H ] in this system.Leo IV has a luminosity of 8700 L _ , suggesting a factor of 40 more star formation than in our models for a typical mass-to-light ratio and a Kroupa IMF e.g martin08 if it formed stars for <100 Myr. The system may be a closer match to our models if the period of star formation was longer. brown12 gives the constraint that the spread of stellar ages is less than 2 Gyr.

sources.summary: The paper revises the Leo IV spectroscopic membership sample, removes one RR Lyrae with variable velocities, and says the removals have little impact because the affected stars have large errors.

quotes.quote: We have made slight membership changes to the Simon2007ApJ...670..313S data for the following dSphs: Canes Venatici I, Coma Berenices, Leo IV, Ursa Major I, and Ursa Major II. We have removed RR Lyrae from the following (identified after the original spectroscopic analysis): Canes Venatici I 5 RR Lyrae identified in Kuehn2008ApJ...674L..81K , Coma Berenices 1 star; Musella2009ApJ...695L..83M , Leo IV 1 star; Moretti2009ApJ...699L.125M , and Ursa Major I 3 stars; Garofalo2013ApJ...767...62G . RR Lyrae are pulsating stars with variable velocity; these stars are dSph members, however, without additional phase information we cannot determine the star's bulk velocity. In Ursa Major II, a foreground dwarf has been removed that was identified in follow-up high-resolution spectroscopy analysis Frebel2010ApJ...708..560F . These removals do not have a large impact as the removed stars tend to have large error bars.

sources.summary: The paper notes that Leo IV has too little abundance data for a strong test, with four alpha-abundance stars showing no decline or only a slight decline.

quotes.quote: Limited abundance data is available for Leo IV, which contains only four stars with known [ /Fe ] vargas13 and 13 with [ Fe/H ] brown14 . The four stars with [ /Fe ] abundances are consistent with no decline with increasing [ Fe/H ] or a slight decline. There is insufficient data to conclude whether Leo IV is consistent with the brown14 two-burst model.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Leo IV as an ultra-faint dwarf fossil candidate with an ancient stellar population, mostly early stellar mass assembly, and possible star formation until z 2.

quotes.quote: brown14 found that the stellar populations of five UFDs: Hercules, Leo IV, Ursa Major I, Bootes 1 and Coma Berenices showed similar ages, suggesting that a global event such as reionization truncated their star formation.
For example, Canes Venatici II formed stars until 8-11  Gyr ago, while Hercules and Leo IV formed 90  of their stellar mass by 11-12 Gyr.
Leo IV also shows only one burst, but likely formed stars until z 2 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Leo IV as an ultra-faint dwarf with low Sr/Mg and Ba/Mg, close-to-solar alpha abundance at low metallicity, and possible SNe Ia pollution.

quotes.quote: Our three UFDs, that is Bo otes I, UMa II, and Leo IV, are depleted in Sr and Ba relative to Fe and Mg, with very similar ratios of [ Sr/Mg ] 1.3 and [ Ba/Mg ] 1 on the entire range of their Mg abundances.
In contrast, Leo IV and Bo otes I, which are the brightest of the Local Group UFDs 2012AJ....144....4M , reveal a close-to-solar /Fe ratio at [ Fe/H ] 2.5 .
The low [ /Fe ] value of the S1 star in the Leo IV UFD suggests that this galaxy had a long enough star formation history to be polluted by SNeIa ejecta.

gmw_00002485.overview: The active galaxy 1321+045 is a low-power, compact steep-spectrum radio source located at the center of a massive galaxy cluster [arXiv:1306.5579, arXiv:1311.3682]. It features a core, symmetric radio lobes spanning up to 16 kiloparsecs, and a newly launched 20-parsec jet [arXiv:1311.3682, arXiv:2110.08358].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1321+045 as a low-power CSS radio source at z=0.263, with core-lobe morphology, LEG optical line ratios, and AGN-photoionized gas rather than shocks.

quotes.quote: Low Power Radio Source The radio source 1321+045 (R.A. =13 ^ h 24 ^ m 19 ^ s .7, Decl. = +04 19 07.2 (J2000.0)) belongs to a class of young CSS radio sources fanti95 . It has been observed with MERLIN at 1.6 GHz in 2007 as a part of large sample of low luminosity compact (LLC) sources kunert10a . The position of the central component visible in 1.6 GHz MERLIN image is well correlated with the position of the optical counterpart suggesting it is a radio core (C). The two lobes (E and W) are located on the opposite sides of the core. There is no evidence of jets or hotspots, however, this needs to be confirmed by observations at higher frequency.

The Ninth SDSS Data Release ahn2012 gives the fluxes of the emission lines visible in spectrum of 1321+045 and the calculated line ratios log = 1.15 and log = 0.41 see for definitions butti10 are consistent with 1321+045 being a LEG. We used ITERA Groves10 to generate emission-line diagnostic (or BPT, after baldwin ) diagrams and to compare the emission-line ratios of 1321+045 with predictions for starburst Leith1999,Dopita06,Levesque2010 , dusty and dust-free AGN Groves04a , and shock Allen08 models.

None of the shock models (with and without precursor) reproduce the flux ratios, suggesting that jet-induced shocks are not present in the ISM of 1321+045 or their contribution to the ionization of the optically emitting gas is negligible.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1321+045 as a compact steep-spectrum radio source in a massive X-ray cluster, with FR-like radio morphology, a core, lobes, and cluster-scale X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The 1321+045 radio source presented here is the second compact steep spectrum radio source known to be associated with a large X-ray cluster. It has a radio morphology different from that of 3C 186 (FR I vs FR II-like) and it is much less luminous at radio waves. These two cases indicate that the radio source properties may be different even though they are located in a similar cluster environment. They provide a great opportunity to investigate the radio source evolution and interaction with the cluster environment.

Diffuse X-ray emission from a cluster of galaxies was only found around one of the sources in our sample, 1321+045 (see Fig. fig2 , left panel). The position of the central bright component visible in the 1.6 GHz MERLIN image of this source is well correlated with the position of the optical counterpart suggesting this component is the radio core, on opposite sides of which there is diffuse emission from two radio lobes (Fig. 2, right panel). This rather symmetric structure has a total length of 17 kpc. The Chandra observations provided an X-ray luminosity of 10^ 42.3 erg/s (Table tab1 ), a cluster temperature kT = 4.4^ +0.5 _ 0.3 keV and an X-ray morphology typical of other massive, relaxed clusters.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1321+045 as a cluster-central CSS radio galaxy with 16 kpc FR I lobes, a young 20 pc VLBA jet, and uncertain recent jet history.

quotes.quote: A third cluster-central CSS radio galaxy is 1321+045, in the z =0.263 cluster MaxBCG J201.08197+04.31863. The radio source has a projected size 4 ^ ( 16 kpc) with an FR I morphology and a clearly separated core Kunert-Bajraszewskaetal10 .

Figure fig:radio shows radio continuum images of the source from the Multi-Element Radio Linked Interferometer Network (MERLIN) at 1.6 GHz, Very Large Array (VLA) at 4.9 GHz, and Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) at 7.5 GHz. The VLA image confirms the same structure as found by Kunert-Bajraszewskaetal10 from the MERLIN data, with east and west lobes (E and W) and a core (C) coincident with the BCG optical centroid. Modelling of the 74 MHz - 4.9 GHz spectrum of the source (including lobes and core) shows a break at 147 ^ +39 _ 36 MHz, which for the measured equipartition magnetic field strength of the source, 150 G, implies a lobe age of 2.0 ^ +0.3 _ 0.2 Myr.

The spectrum shows no sign of the high-frequency break expected if the jets no longer power the lobes, implying that if they have ceased to do so, it is only in the last < 0.11 Myr. The VLBA image probes much smaller angular scales and reveals a 5 mas ( 20 pc) extension of the core component. We interpret this as a jet, with the difference between its projected axis and that of the radio lobes suggesting that it may be newly launched.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1321+045 as a young cluster-central CSS radio galaxy with old 5 kpc lobes, a newly launched 20 pc jet, and cooling-core feedback signatures.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 1321+045 is a rare example of a young, compact steep spectrum source located in the center of a z =0.263 galaxy cluster. Using a combination of , VLBA, VLA, MERLIN and IRAM 30m observations, we investigate the conditions which have triggered this outburst. We find that the previously identified 5 kpc scale radio lobes are probably no longer powered by the AGN, which seems to have launched a new 20 pc jet on a different axis, likely within the last few hundred years.

The radio source 1321 + 045 (R.A. =13 ^ h 24 ^ m 19 ^ s .7, Decl. = +04 19 07.2 , J2000.0) belongs to the class of young CSS radio sources. It has been observed with MERLIN at 1.6 GHz in 2006 December as a part of a large sample of low luminosity compact (LLC) sources Kunert-Bajraszewskaetal10 .
The total projected length of the source is 16 kpc and its radio luminosity, L_ 5 GHz 10^ 25 W  Hz^ 1 ( < 10^ 42 erg  s^ 1 ), places it in the FR I-FR II transition region.

gmw_00002486.name: PBC J1546.5+6931
gmw_00002486.overview: PBC J1546.5+6931 is a galaxy characterized by a bimodal stellar population [arXiv:1306.5759]. A younger population with an age of 3.6 Gyr contributes 70 percent of the galaxy's total mass, while an older, subsolar metallicity population makes up the remaining 30 percent [arXiv:1306.5759].

sources.summary: The paper finds PBC J1546.5+6931 has a bimodal stellar population: a 3.6 Gyr component contributes 70 percent of its mass, while an older subsolar component makes up the rest.

quotes.quote: There are only 2 galaxies (PBC J1546.5+6931 and PBC J0041.6+2534) with t/Gyr _M < 7 . However, PBC J1546.5+6931 shows a clear bimodality in the distribution and, for this reason, t/Gyr _M and _M are not properly describing its stellar population. Therefore, for this object we independently derived the typical parameters of the two distinct stellar populations. The younger one is characterized by t/Gyr _M=3.6 and _M=0.18 and it contributes to 70 of the total mass of the galaxy. The remaining 30 of the galaxy mass is due to a older population ( t/Gyr _M=14.8 ) with subsolar metallicity ( _M=-0.27 ).

On the other hand, this is not the case of PBC J1546.5+6931 which is showing a dominant young population and whose linear scale is bigger ( 2 kpc). An explanation for the global young stellar population of this galaxy must be searched in its formation and evolutionary history.

gmw_00002487.name: PBC J0919.9+3712
gmw_00002487.overview: The galaxy PBC J0919.9+3712 contains a young secondary stellar population [arXiv:1306.5759]. This younger component accounts for 12 percent of the galaxy's total mass and is located within its compact nuclear region [arXiv:1306.5759].

sources.summary: The paper reports PBC J0919.9+3712 has a young secondary stellar population comprising 12 percent of the galaxy mass, plausibly detected because the observations probe a compact nuclear region.

quotes.quote: Even though this is the most representative example of bimodality in the stellar population distribution, there are other two galaxies, namely PBC J0919.9+3712 and PBC J1345.4+4141, showing a second stellar population accounting for more than 10 of the total galaxy mass. The final results for the three galaxies with two separate stellar populations are listed in Table Tab:results2comp . As can be noted from Table Tab:results2comp , for both PBC J0919.9+3712 and PBC J1345.4+4141 the less massive component is a young one, which accounts for 12 and 15 of the total mass, respectively. The linear scales for these two objects are the smallest in our sample ( 200 pc) and this could have increased our sensibility in detecting a young component existing in the nuclear region of the galaxy.
This could be the reason for the young component detected in PBC J0919.9+3712 and PBC J1345.4+4141.

gmw_00002488.name: PBC J1345.4+4141
gmw_00002488.overview: The galaxy NGC 5290, also known as PBC J1345.4+4141, features an asymmetric, dusty disk embedded in a thicker outer component [arXiv:astro-ph/0703768]. Its nuclear region hosts a young secondary stellar population [arXiv:1306.5759] and a central radio source with a GHz-peaked spectrum [arXiv:2312.04452].

sources.summary: The paper reports PBC J1345.4+4141 has a young secondary stellar population comprising 15 percent of the galaxy mass, plausibly detected because the observations probe a compact nuclear region.

quotes.quote: Even though this is the most representative example of bimodality in the stellar population distribution, there are other two galaxies, namely PBC J0919.9+3712 and PBC J1345.4+4141, showing a second stellar population accounting for more than 10 of the total galaxy mass. The final results for the three galaxies with two separate stellar populations are listed in Table Tab:results2comp . As can be noted from Table Tab:results2comp , for both PBC J0919.9+3712 and PBC J1345.4+4141 the less massive component is a young one, which accounts for 12 and 15 of the total mass, respectively. The linear scales for these two objects are the smallest in our sample ( 200 pc) and this could have increased our sensibility in detecting a young component existing in the nuclear region of the galaxy.
This could be the reason for the young component detected in PBC J0919.9+3712 and PBC J1345.4+4141.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5290 as a dusty, asymmetric disk with a thicker outer component, side-to-side profile differences, and a face-on-equivalent profile fit in two bands.

quotes.quote: NGC 5290: The inner disk is embedded in an outer, clearly thicker disk component or in a low surface brightness extension of the bulge. The galaxy exhibits a thick and patchy dustlane, and the two sides are rather asymmetric. In the contour map the E(left)-side seems to be thicker and shorter, whereas the W(right)-side is longer and tapers off. This asymmetry is reflected in the major axis radial profile. When fitted with a broken exponential structure, the slope of the outer region on the E(left)-side is much steeper compared to the W(right)-side.

sources.summary: The paper presents sub-parsec VLBA imaging and radio SED results for NGC 5290, describing its central radio morphology and spectral shape in the FRAMEx AGN sample.

quotes.quote: NGC 5290 [ 13 ^ h 45 ^ m 19 16076, + 41 42 44 463 ] : All four band images show a bright point source at the center. The SED sub-plots using only C and X bands show a similar flat spectrum, supporting the idea of a point source. The four-frequency SED plot shows a GHz-peaked spectrum with a peak at 6.70 GHz. A similar radio peak intensity drop was seen at lower and higher frequency ends.

gmw_00002489.name: 2MASX J1439+1415
gmw_00002489.overview: 2MASX J1439+1415 is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1409.0905] classified as an optically elusive active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1409.0905]. Its optical normalcy is likely caused by heavy starlight dilution from the host galaxy [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper interprets 2MASX J1439+1415 as a high-redshift XBONG whose optical normalcy is most likely host-galaxy starlight dilution, with elliptical morphology and Type 1-like indicators.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J1439+1415 This is our highest redshift object: the SDSS spectroscopic fiber contains 62 of the total galaxy light. It has a high Eddington ratio, low X-ray column density, and an elliptical morphology which generally implies a small dust component. The optical spectrum shows a striking resemblance to the starlight-diluted artificial spectra of moran02 ; in particular, the presence of H but lack of H and very weak . While these features are also present in the other XBONG spectra, those objects have high X-ray column densities and low Eddington ratios, and are much nearer, so the spectroscopic fiber encompasses only 20 of the total galaxy light.

It passes all three NIR photometric tests. Additionally, its 4000 break strength is quite high, consistent with heavy starlight dilution. Finally, its NIR continuum fit resembles the optically elusive AGN in our sample, rather than that of its fellow XBONGs. We conclude that this object is most likely optically normal due to dilution of the spectrum by host galaxy starlight; the object would likely be classified as a Type 1 AGN if it were nearer or the spectrum excluded more of the host galaxy.

gmw_00002490.name: 2MFGC 00829
gmw_00002490.overview: 2MFGC 00829 is an edge-on spiral galaxy classified as an X-ray bright optically normal galaxy [arXiv:1409.0905]. It lacks optical or near-infrared emission lines, and its active nucleus is likely obscured by widespread dust within the host galaxy [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2MFGC 00829 as an edge-on spiral XBONG with little ancillary data, no optical or NIR emission lines, and likely dust obscuration from its host galaxy.

quotes.quote: 2MFGC 00829 This XBONG is difficult to study because of a lack of information; it is not in the SDSS, and so we do not have its U-R color. The only available image is from 2MASS, which is lower resolution. The low quality X-ray spectrum prevents us from constraining its line-of-sight column density. There is no Spitzer data for this object, so we cannot look for silicate absorption troughs.

Since it is not at high redshift, we think starlight dilution is unlikely. Since it is an XBONG, not an elusive AGN, and has no emission lines at all, nuclear star formation is not an explanation. Our best guess for this object is dust obscuration, perhaps due to widespread dust in the host galaxy, since it appears to be an edge-on spiral. The fit of its NIR continuum is consistent with the dust-obscured XBONGs, in that the hot nuclear dust component is quite small compared to the stellar blackbody component, implying further obscuration of the nucleus but a cold dust component further away.

gmw_00002491.name: KUG 1238+278A
gmw_00002491.overview: KUG 1238+278A is an interacting compact galaxy that is morphologically disturbed [arXiv:1409.0905]. Near-infrared observations reveal a hidden broad-line active galactic nucleus seen through weak dust obscuration, alongside a potential nuclear starburst [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper identifies KUG 1238+278A as an optically elusive, interacting compact galaxy whose NIR spectrum reveals a hidden broad-line AGN seen through weaker dust obscuration.

quotes.quote: KUG 1238+278A This object's NIR spectrum is dominated by nuclear emission. We see broad Paschen lines and the flat continuum associated with AGN, a classical hidden broad line object. The reason that it falls in the star forming region of optical diagnostic plots may be due to a nuclear starburst, possibly fueled by an ongoing merger; its image suggests it may be morphologically disturbed by an interaction. It is classified as a compact galaxy in the 2MASS NIR galaxy morphology atlas of jarrett00 , which they describe as ``not obviously spiral or elliptical." Its NIR continuum fit is similar to the other optically elusive AGN, consisting of relatively equal contributions from a stellar blackbody and a hot dust component.

It passes all three NIR photometric tests. Dust obscuration must play some role, because if we penetrate the dust by observing in the NIR, its AGN nature becomes clear; however, this dust obscuration must be weaker than that affecting the XBONGs, since their NIR spectra remain those of inactive galaxies.

gmw_00002492.name: Mrk 18
gmw_00002492.overview: Mrk 18 is a newly recognized active galactic nucleus first discovered in the hard X-ray band [arXiv:1409.0905]. It exhibits strong polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon features and nuclear starburst activity, which likely affects its optical emission line ratios [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 18 as a newly recognized hard-X-ray AGN whose optical line ratios are likely affected by nuclear star formation, with PAH features and infrared AGN diagnostics.

quotes.quote: Mrk 18 Mrk 18 is a previously unknown AGN, first discovered in the hard X-ray, and weaver10 suggest this is because of ``extinction of the optical emission lines." winter10 classify it as an HII region / LINER, as we do. Our strongest candidate for nuclear starburst activity, this object exhibits strong PAH features associated with star formation in its Spitzer spectrum. It is unlikely to be seriously affected by dust obscuration, since its E(B-V) value is quite low (0.05) and the Spitzer spectrum does not show silicate absorption troughs. It has numerous AGN diagnostics in the infrared, including the detection of MIR high ionization lines and , as well as NIR emission line ratio diagnostics.

Finally, Mrk 18 falls in the hybrid SB/AGN portion of the genzel98 MIR classification diagram. We believe that, rather than heavy extinction, this object's line ratios are affected by a nuclear starburst, causing it to fail classical optical AGN ratio tests. Some nuclear dust may slightly reduce the AGN contribution to the optical lines with respect to the NIR lines, since the object passes NIR line diagnostics.

gmw_00002493.name: NGC 4686
gmw_00002493.overview: NGC 4686 is classified as a radio-quiet, X-ray bright optically normal galaxy [arXiv:1409.0905]. Its optical normalcy is attributed to dust obscuration, and the galaxy features a dust lane as well as a high X-ray column density [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 4686 as a radio-quiet XBONG whose optical normalcy is best explained by dust obscuration, high X-ray column density, silicate absorption, and a dust lane.

quotes.quote: NGC 4686 This XBONG is almost certainly optically normal due to dust obscuration. ajello09 classify this object as a radio-quiet AGN of the XBONG type. It has a high X-ray column density and its Spitzer spectrum exhibits silicate absorption, although not to the same extreme degree as NGC 4992. We do not significantly detect in the NIR spectrum, but we do detect in the MIR spectrum (see Figure fig:spitzer ), which is an unambiguous AGN indicator.

Since NGC 4686 is not an SDSS spectroscopic target and its spectrum does not extend blueward enough to contain the 4000 break, we cannot estimate the amount of starlight dilution present in our optical spectrum from Kitt Peak. The NIR spectrum, like the optical, has no emission lines and the NIR continuum fit is consistent with a stellar population with a minimal hot dust contribution, probably shielded by colder dust. The obscuration may be due to host galaxy dust, since a dust lane is clearly present in the image.

gmw_00002494.name: NGC 5610
gmw_00002494.overview: NGC 5610 is a nearby face-on barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1409.0905]. It is classified as a radio-quiet active galactic nucleus that may host a nuclear starburst, though its optical diagnostics are dominated by starburst or HII region emission lines due to dust blocking the nucleus [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5610 as a face-on barred spiral, optically elusive radio-quiet AGN whose optical diagnostics may be dominated by a nuclear starburst while dust blocks the nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 5610 This face-on barred spiral galaxy is optically elusive and classifies as a starburst / HII region on optical line ratio diagnostic diagrams. Like with NGC 4686, ajello09 call it a radio-quiet AGN of the XBONG type. We do not believe starlight dilution is a viable explanation for the optical normalcy of this object for several reasons: it is nearby ( z=0.0168 ), the SDSS spectrum is quite nuclear, and the strength of the 4000 break is consistent with Type 2 AGN and even some Type 1 AGN (see Figure fig:4000a ). It does not have an unusually low Eddington ratio.

Since we do not have a MIR spectrum for this object, we do not know if it exhibits PAH features or silicate absorption. Its face-on orientation, however, precludes dust absorption on large scales in the host galaxy along the line of sight. So, this object possibly hosts a nuclear starburst, the emission lines from which dominate the emission lines from gas ionized by the AGN itself due to dust blocking the nucleus.

gmw_00002495.name: UGC 05881
gmw_00002495.overview: UGC 05881 is a barred spiral galaxy classified as a LINER and an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1409.0905]. Its near-infrared continuum is dominated by a stellar component, with slight nuclear dust obscuration and local reddening present in its central regions [arXiv:1409.0905].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 05881 as a barred spiral LINER/AGN with local reddening, successful NIR line diagnostics, a mostly stellar NIR continuum, and slight nuclear dust obscuration.

quotes.quote: UGC 05881 In addition to the Swift BAT survey, UGC 05881 was also detected by the IBIS instrument on INTEGRAL . masetti10 classify the INTEGRAL source optically as a low-ionization nuclear emission line region (LINER) and as Compton-thin. Interestingly, those authors claim that there is no reddening local to the AGN host, and thus declare it a ``naked" LINER, which is the first of its kind. The optical classification of LINER is not entirely inconsistent with our optical classification as an HII region, since the object falls in the composite regime between star formation and AGN-dominated; i.e., between the kewley06 and kauffmann03 demarcations. We do, however, find that there is local reddening on the order of E(B-V) 0.25 .

The object is successfully classified as an AGN using NIR emission line diagnostics; therefore we believe there is some slight nuclear dust obscuration causing it to fail optical AGN diagnostics. Interestingly, the NIR continuum fit for this object resembles the XBONGs, rather than the optically elusive AGN, in that it is best matched by a predominant stellar component and only a small hot dust contribution. Perhaps the slight obscuration of this object is due to large-scale colder dust in the central regions of this barred spiral galaxy.

gmw_00002496.name: VGS 05
gmw_00002496.overview: VGS 05 is an early-type void galaxy with a red color and a central disk or bar structure [arXiv:1204.5185]. It has no detected neutral hydrogen [arXiv:1609.04920, arXiv:1204.5185], lacks companions [arXiv:1609.04920], and exhibits the lowest specific star formation rate in the Void Galaxy Survey [arXiv:1204.5185].

sources.summary: The paper highlights VGS 05 as a rare early-type void galaxy in the deepest underdensity, with high stellar mass-to-light ratio, no HI detection, no companions, and possibly accelerated evolution.

quotes.quote: Perhaps the most surprising finding is that of an early-type galaxy, VGS 05, which lies in the deepest underdensity of the entire sample, at 0.93 . VGS 05 is indeed a rarity amongst the void galaxies. It has a stellar mass-to-light ratio of 6.8 in 3.6 m , is not detected in H i , and has no detected companion, neither in the optical nor in H i . The presence of this galaxy in the sample is interesting, as it is the only example of this morphological type. Considering its high stellar mass-to-light ratio, it must have evolved much faster than the rest of the sample and consumed its H i .

On the other hand one of the three galaxies (VGS 05) in the VGS with stellar mass of the order of 10^ 10 M_ , is an elliptical galaxy with no star formation activity detected either in optical or near-infrared and not detected in H i . The other two VGS galaxies are star forming spirals. This raises the question how do void galaxies acquire their mass and can voids harbour galaxies as massive as those found in dense environments? Massive ellipticals are believed to accumulate mass via interactions and major mergers whereas disk galaxies via star formation and accretion of small satellites sch2000,kauffmann2003,kauffmann2004,dok2005 . Given its stellar mass of 2 10^ 10 M_ , VGS 05 is not a typical massive elliptical galaxy but an early-type galaxy that reside very close to the centre of its parent void ( 0.93 ). Is VGS 05 an example of secular evolution or was it subject to interactions and major mergers very early in time compared to the rest of 58 void galaxies in the VGS sample?

sources.summary: The paper identifies VGS 05 as an H I non-detection with early-type optical morphology, red color, central disk-or-bar structure, and the survey's lowest specific star formation rate.

quotes.quote: VGS 05 is not detected in H i , and has an early type optical morphology. Red in color ( g- = 0.76 ), it does not present an entirely uniform elliptical morphology, with the light distribution in the center suggesting the presence of a disk or bar. It has the lowest specific star formation rate of the VGS.

gmw_00002497.name: VGS 12
gmw_00002497.overview: VGS 12 is an isolated polar disk galaxy located in a tenuous wall between two voids [arXiv:1609.04920]. It features a central stellar disk that is actively forming stars [arXiv:1101.4187] and a perpendicular, star-poor HI disk extending nearly 30 kpc [arXiv:1410.6597, arXiv:1101.4187].

sources.summary: The paper describes VGS 12 as an isolated small void galaxy with a blue stellar disk and a nearly 30 kpc perpendicular HI disk, interpreted as cold gas accretion.

quotes.quote: The strongest example we have that presents evidence for accretion out of the void is VGS 12 (Figure fig2 , ). This small stellar system presents a blue, disk-like morphology that extends to about 5 kpc in diameter. The HI emission also presents a regular disk-like morphology and kinematics, however it is oriented perpendicular to the stellar component, and extends over nearly 30 kpc. This sort of polar disk configuration has been seen before in other galaxies, most notably NGC 4650A. A satellite accretion or interaction is typically understood to be required for the formation of such disjoint kinematic components in one galaxy.

However, this is not the case for VGS 12, where we see no evidence for a stellar component in the polar disk through deep R-band imaging. Because of this, the isolated nature of this system, and the apparently undisturbed nature of the central stellar disk, we believe that this void galaxy presents strong evidence for ongoing cold accretion of gas onto this system. This confirms predictions from simulations that such a mode of galaxy growth plays an important role in low mass halos ( ), but which has proven difficult to isolate in observations.

sources.summary: The paper presents VGS 12 as an isolated polar disk/ring galaxy on a wall between voids, with an immense perpendicular HI disk and structural parameters comparable to other VGS galaxies.

quotes.quote: One of these is a polar ring galaxy, VGS 12, located in a tenuous wall between two voids stanonik2009 .

The other interesting case is that of VGS 12, a polar disk galaxy for an extensive study see stanonik2009 . It is located in a tenuous wall that forms the boundary between two walls, and is amongst the most isolated objects in the nearby Universe. Even though its colour, stellar mass and star formation activity are not dissimilar from other typical void galaxies, such as VGS 20 and VGS 41, it has the uniquely interesting feature of an immense H i disk rotating perpendicular to its stellar disk. This disk may hint at the influence of its special location, which may be responsible for the cold inflow of gas from the two voids on whose boundary it is located stanonik2009 .

In terms of structural parameters, VGS 12 has a half-light radius (r_ e )_ B 1.2 kpc and a surface brightness ( _ e )_ B 23 mag/arcsec^ 2 . These are comparable to the VGS sample in general. However, it has a significantly smaller S e rsic index n 0.7 than the mean n =1.1 of the VGS sample.

sources.summary: The paper describes VGS12 as a lonely void polar disk galaxy whose massive star-poor perpendicular HI disk and undisturbed stellar disk suggest slow cold gas accretion rather than a merger.

quotes.quote: An outstanding specimen of our sample is the polar disk galaxy VGS12 . Amongst the most lonely galaxies in the universe, it has a massive, star-poor HI disk that is perpendicular to the disk of the central void galaxy. No optical counterpart to the HI disk has yet been found, even though the inner optical galaxy is actively forming stars.

The galaxy is located within a tenuous wall in between two large roundish voids. The undisrupted appearance of the original stellar disk renders a merger origin unlikely. It suggests slow accretion of cold gas , at the crossing point of the outflow from the two voids.

sources.summary: The paper describes VGS 12 as a massive polar-ring void galaxy whose extended, star-poor H i disk and rotationally supported stellar disk favor slow gas accretion over a major merger.

quotes.quote: VGS 12 is a polar ring galaxy, and is discussed in detail in . It is one of the most massive of our sample, in both its H i ( 3.0 0.5 10^9 M_ ) and dynamical ( 1.5 10^ 11 M_ ) mass, and is substantially more massive in H i than stellar mass ( 1.05 10^9 M_ ). In addition, its H i disk is extremely extended compared to the optical, yet free of stars down to the SDSS surface brightness limits, ruling out most merger scenarios. Simulations can reproduce such gas-rich, star-poor rings through tidal encounters, however at equal mass ratios they destroy the rotational support of the central galaxy, resulting in the formation of an elliptical remnant Bournaud2005 . The low concentration index, r_ 90 / r_ 50 , of 2.25 implies the central stellar disk is late type Shen2003 and thus rotationally supported, suggesting an alternative formation mechanism such as slow accretion of cold gas is necessary Iodice2006 .

gmw_00002498.name: VGS 20
gmw_00002498.overview: VGS 20 is a very compact void galaxy with one of the smallest half-light radii in the Void Galaxy Survey sample [arXiv:1609.04920]. It is characterized as a very blue object that lacks an HI detection [arXiv:1609.04920].

sources.summary: The paper identifies VGS 20 as a very compact, blue void galaxy lacking HI detection and having one of the smallest half-light radii in the VGS sample.

quotes.quote: VGS 20 and VGS 41 represent what we call compact objects in our sample due to the lack of any distinguishable feature. Both of them are very blue objects without an H i detection.
Two of these are galaxies with the smallest half-light-radius of 0.7 kpc in the VGS sample. They are the very compact galaxies VGS 20 and VGS 41 (Figure fig:morph ).

Even though its colour, stellar mass and star formation activity are not dissimilar from other typical void galaxies, such as VGS 20 and VGS 41, it has the uniquely interesting feature of an immense H i disk rotating perpendicular to its stellar disk.

gmw_00002499.name: VGS 41
gmw_00002499.overview: VGS 41 is a very compact void galaxy with one of the smallest half-light radii in the Void Galaxy Survey sample [arXiv:1609.04920]. It is characterized as a very blue object that lacks any HI detection [arXiv:1609.04920].

sources.summary: The paper identifies VGS 41 as a very compact, blue void galaxy lacking HI detection and having one of the smallest half-light radii in the VGS sample.

quotes.quote: VGS 20 and VGS 41 represent what we call compact objects in our sample due to the lack of any distinguishable feature. Both of them are very blue objects without an H i detection.
Two of these are galaxies with the smallest half-light-radius of 0.7 kpc in the VGS sample. They are the very compact galaxies VGS 20 and VGS 41 (Figure fig:morph ).

Even though its colour, stellar mass and star formation activity are not dissimilar from other typical void galaxies, such as VGS 20 and VGS 41, it has the uniquely interesting feature of an immense H i disk rotating perpendicular to its stellar disk.

gmw_00002500.name: VGS 31
gmw_00002500.overview: VGS 31 is a system of three interacting void galaxies [arXiv:1609.04920, arXiv:1601.08228] aligned along a tenuous filament [arXiv:1609.04920, arXiv:1410.6597] and embedded in a common neutral hydrogen envelope [arXiv:1609.04920, arXiv:1204.5185]. The system shows signs of minor mergers [arXiv:1609.04920], triggered star formation [arXiv:1609.04920], and cold gas accretion [arXiv:1410.6597].

sources.summary: The paper discusses VGS 31 as a three-galaxy interacting void system aligned with a tenuous filament, embedded in a common HI envelope, and showing minor merger and triggered star-formation signs.

quotes.quote: We also found a configuration of three void galaxies, VGS 31, aligned along what appears to be a tenuous filament along a void beygu2013,rieder2013 .

When considering the position of void galaxies within the context of the overall structural complexity of the cosmic web, the two VGS galaxies with the most outstanding locations are VGS 31 beygu2013 and VGS 12 stanonik2009 . VGS 31 is a system of three interacting system aligned along a filamentary feature in the interior of a void. It is likely that this system, discussed in detail in , reflects a density enhancement in an underlying tenuous dark matter filament.

Two such examples are VGS 31 and VGS 38. VGS 31 is a group of three interacting galaxies embedded in the same H i envelope see for details beygu2013 . The group also shows sign for minor merger and triggered star formation activity. We explored the dynamical and environmental origin and development of VGS 31 in some detail rieder2013 .

sources.summary: The paper presents VGS31 as a three-galaxy void system spanning 57 kpc, likely linked by an HI bridge and showing streams and tails suggestive of recent satellite infall.

quotes.quote: Another fascinating object is VGS31. It defines a system of three galaxies, stretching out over 57 kpc and possibly connected by a HI bridge.

The easternmost object is a Markarian galaxy, marked by prominent stellar streams wrapping around the central galaxy and a separate tidal tail or stream (see fig. fig:vgs31 ). These might be the remnants of the recent infall of one or two satellite galaxies . The westernmost object is considerably fainter than the other two galaxies. The tails and streams are also visible in the recent deep INT B imaging as well as on the NIR J and K maps, possibly with a slight and unique misalignment.

sources.summary: The paper presents VGS 31 as three galaxies aligned in a void filament, connected by a smooth HI bridge and showing evidence for cold gas accretion.

quotes.quote: A second example of this is the VGS 31 system, which contains three galaxies that appear to be forming along a filament within the void (Figure fig3 , ). All three are linearly aligned and at nearly identical velocity, and they are connected by a tenuous HI bridge with relatively smooth gas kinematics connecting all three systems. The entire filamentary configuration extends nearly 100 kpc across the sky.

Simulations designed to reproduce such a configuration show that it is likely this system is assembling within a (proto)filament and not just a group of interacting galaxies ( ). Further observational evidence for this is seen in the low metallicity of an outlying polar disk of material surrounding the eastern-most galaxy, suggesting again that this system presents evidence for cold accretion of gas ( ). The VGS 31 system presents a fascinating example of the possible formation of substructure within a void.

sources.summary: SIT 264 G2 is described as an irregular/late spiral candidate with elongated structure, bright ring, faint eastern tail, and distorted-spiral residual appearance.

quotes.quote: The irregular galaxy, G2 , has an elongated structure without sub-components e.g. bar, arms, or disk components.

The intensity profile of G2 (Figure 264_SB , middle panel) exhibits a strong evidence of an extremely bright ring located between 3 '' < SMA < 6.5 '' after which a hump of type III-d appears in the inner disk, while the outer region of the disk represents type I profile with no breaks.

The irregular shape of G2 makes its decomposition very difficult with a poor residual image. A two-component decomposition ( Ser + Exp ) reveals an over-subtracted faint tail at the Eastern portion with some dark patches in the central region (Figure 264G2resid2 ). Even by summing a Dev model to the Ser and Exp models, some features remain unfitted in the residual image with ^2_ of 1.41. The residual image of this galaxy match a distorted spiral galaxy more than an irregular galaxy. In addition, both A and S values of this galaxy confirm that it is a late type spiral galaxy as estimated by .

sources.summary: The paper identifies VGS 31a as a Markarian starburst outlier with high star formation, known molecular content, and elevated star-formation efficiency relative to similar-mass VGS galaxies.

quotes.quote: VGS 31 and 31a are two Markarian galaxies and have the highest SFRs of the whole sample. They occupy a region overlapping with starbursts.
Since we do not know the H_ 2 content of our galaxies (except VGS 31a beygu2013 ) and our H i data is not good enough for a spatial KS law analysis we examined the global KS law for the VGS galaxies.

When corrected for extinction both the SFR /M_ HI and SFR /M_ * of the VGS galaxies and the comparison sample galaxies as a function of M_ * (Figure figure:12 ) are very similar, except that two VGS galaxies around M_ * = 10^ 10 M_ stand out with higher SFR /M_ HI (Figure figure:12 a, top panel). These are VGS 31a and VGS 57.

sources.summary: The paper describes VGS 31 as a three-galaxy system with two H I-rich companions, a common H I envelope and bridge, and interaction signs including a gas-poor stellar ring.

quotes.quote: VGS 31 is the second void galaxy in our sample to have two H i rich companions, with all three sharing a common envelope in H i .
