quotes.quote: The objects SDSS J093844, J095036, and J161911 are of interest since they have been highlighted by runnoe17 as the most promising SBHB candidates in the sample, based on the properties of their radial velocity curves. More specifically, the radial velocity curves of these candidates show a statistically significant monotonic change in radial velocity over the duration of observations. As shown in Figure fig:cutoff , the nearest neighbor profiles in the synthetic database provide a good description of the observed profiles for J093844, J095036, and J161911, as reflected by their average quality indices, QI = 0.44 , 0.59 and 0.69, respectively.

Among the three candidates, J095036 (middle) is characterized by the smallest degree of degeneracy in the inferred values of (a/M) and q , as witnessed by their values of entropy listed in Appendix sec:ParamTable ( S _a S _q = 0.44 ). This is because the properties of the profiles of J095036 (such as the location of the peak and profile asymmetry), show significant change from one observation to another.

The same property is more degenerate for J095036 (middle), which, as noted earlier, is the candidate with the narrowest emission-line profiles among the three. This can be understood as the disk wind has less effect on the shapes of narrower profiles, and hence, accretion disk wind with a range of optical depths can produce profile shapes similar to the relatively narrow profiles of J095036.

sources.summary: The paper treats J0950+5128 as one of three pilot variable AGN with rising high-frequency fluxes; follow-up found roughly steady X-band flux over weeks with a slight possible increase.

quotes.quote: Because a preliminary analysis of our first three pilot sources (J0807+2102, J0832+2302, and J0950+5128) showed rising fluxes at high frequencies, we modified our observing strategy to add higher frequency Ku band observations (12--18 GHz) for the remaining sample, and returned later to our three pilot sources on September 19, 2019 to repeat the X band observations and add Ku band. We discuss the X-band variability on weeks timescale for these three sources in Section  sec:variability_timescale .

At X band, the two observing epochs shown in Figure  fig:timeseries for J0807+2102, J0832+2302, and J0950+5128 span an even narrower range of time ( weeks) compared to the variability timescale constraints at L and S band. The X band flux densities are roughly constant, although all 3 sources exhibit a slight increase in flux of 10 over a period of 58 days. Although an increase in flux density of this magnitude could be due to the evolution of a nascent jet (see Section  sec:origin ), the presence of residual errors in the absolute flux scale of similar magnitude cannot be entirely ruled out.

sources.summary: J095036 is one of three monitored SBHB candidates with monotonic velocity changes of several hundred km/s, making it a promising high-priority target for continued spectroscopy and host-galaxy imaging.

quotes.quote: The above cautions notwithstanding, we would like to draw attention to three objects in our sample, J093844, J095036, and J161911, which have shown systematic and monotonic velocity changes by several hundred kms s ^ 1 . These represent the most promising cases in our sample and deserve further attention. They are high-priority targets for continued spectroscopic monitoring as well as additional observational tests, such as high-resolution imaging of the host galaxies in search of morphological features associated with a merger.

Based on our spectroscopic monitoring, of these 10 objects there are three (J093844, J095036, J161911) in which the radial velocity curves show monotonic variations by many hundreds of kms s ^ 1 , incompatible with a single BLR bound to a recoiling BH.

gmw_00002545.name: J161911
gmw_00002545.overview: J161911 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1910.02412] that is considered a promising supermassive binary black hole candidate [arXiv:1908.01799, arXiv:1702.05465]. Its radial velocity curves show systematic, monotonic variations of several hundred kilometers per second [arXiv:1702.05465], and it has a relatively high inferred mass ratio [arXiv:1908.01799].

sources.summary: The paper models J161911 as a promising SBHB candidate with good profile matches, high inferred mass ratio, comparable mini-disk fluxes, and relatively constrained optical depth.

quotes.quote: The objects SDSS J093844, J095036, and J161911 are of interest since they have been highlighted by runnoe17 as the most promising SBHB candidates in the sample, based on the properties of their radial velocity curves. More specifically, the radial velocity curves of these candidates show a statistically significant monotonic change in radial velocity over the duration of observations. As shown in Figure fig:cutoff , the nearest neighbor profiles in the synthetic database provide a good description of the observed profiles for J093844, J095036, and J161911, as reflected by their average quality indices, QI = 0.44 , 0.59 and 0.69, respectively.

Figure fig:individualtauF2F1 shows the PDFs for the same three SBHB candidates in terms of the optical depth parameter, _0 , and the ratio of the H emission-line flux contributed by the secondary and primary mini-disk, F_2/F_1 . The value of the optical depth parameter is relatively well constrained for J093844 (left panel) and J161911 (right) and it peaks at _0 1 .

From the three binary candidates considered in this section, J093844 (left) and J161911 (right) have relatively high inferred mass ratios, q 0.7 , and consequently, the fluxes emitted by the two mini-disks are comparable.

sources.summary: J161911 is one of three monitored SBHB candidates with monotonic velocity changes of several hundred km/s, making it a promising high-priority target for continued spectroscopy and host-galaxy imaging.

quotes.quote: The above cautions notwithstanding, we would like to draw attention to three objects in our sample, J093844, J095036, and J161911, which have shown systematic and monotonic velocity changes by several hundred kms s ^ 1 . These represent the most promising cases in our sample and deserve further attention. They are high-priority targets for continued spectroscopic monitoring as well as additional observational tests, such as high-resolution imaging of the host galaxies in search of morphological features associated with a merger.

Based on our spectroscopic monitoring, of these 10 objects there are three (J093844, J095036, J161911) in which the radial velocity curves show monotonic variations by many hundreds of kms s ^ 1 , incompatible with a single BLR bound to a recoiling BH.

sources.summary: The paper selects SDSS J161911.24+501109.2 as a monitored AGN, gives its luminosity, H-beta width, black-hole mass and Eddington ratio, and measures its reverberation lag.

quotes.quote: Based on cross-correlation analysis using B band and H emission line light curves, we measured the rest-frame lag of 33.2^ +3.9 _ 4.7 and 42.6^ +14.3 _ 11.9 days, respectively for 2MASS J1026 and SDSS J1619. Using the _ line of the total H line profile in the rms spectrum and a virial factor of f=4.47 , we determined black hole mass as 3.65^ +0.49 _ 0.57 10^7 M_ ( 23.02^ +7.81 _ 6.56 10^7 M_ ) for 2MASS J1026 (SDSS J1619).

gmw_00002546.name: NGC 2782
gmw_00002546.overview: NGC 2782 is a peculiar spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0703493, arXiv:2002.07312] that hosts a double-barred structure [arXiv:0802.2775], a nuclear starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0703493, arXiv:astro-ph/0408383], and an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2208.05848]. It features an eastern stellar tail and a western neutral hydrogen tail [arXiv:1201.3927, arXiv:2002.07312] that were likely triggered by a past galaxy merger [arXiv:1201.3927, arXiv:astro-ph/0703493].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 2782 as a double-barred, previously interacting starburst galaxy where stellar torques and decoupled nuclear bars drive molecular gas inward toward the nucleus.

quotes.quote: The CO emission is aligned along the stellar nuclear bar of radius 1 kpc, configured in an elongated structure with two spiral arms at high pitch angle 90^ . At the extremity of the nuclear bar, the CO changes direction to trace two more extended spiral features at a lower pitch angle. These are the beginning of two straight dust lanes, which are aligned parallel to an oval distortion, reminiscent of a primary bar, almost perpendicular to the nuclear one.

Main features Although the outer parts of NGC 2782 show clear signs of a past interaction (tidal tails, distorted disk, stellar sheets), the inner parts appear dynamically relaxed, not unexpectedly since the dynamical timescale is shorter by one or two orders of magnitude at 1kpc, than at 20kpc. The previous sections have shown that \ contains many features of a double-barred galaxy. Although the nuclear bar is unfortunately almost aligned with the galaxy line of nodes, the disk is nearly face-on (30 ^ ), which helps to distinguish the morphological features.

A primary bar, perhaps triggered by the past interaction, has driven gas toward its ILR, and while this bar was weakening, a secondary embedded bar has decoupled, prolonging the gas inflow inside the ILR ring, which is now conspicuous in star formation and H emission.

sources.summary: The paper presents NUGA molecular-gas results for NGC 2782, showing CO aligned with its nuclear bar, negative inner torques, and nested bar resonances linked to gas inflow.

quotes.quote: Figure n2782 shows the CO morphology found for the starburst/Seyfert 1 NGC 2782 leslie08 . The ^ 12 CO(1-0) emission is aligned with the nuclear stellar bar of radius 1 kpc. At the ends of the nuclear bar, the CO changes direction and traces diffuse asymmetric spiral arms extending to the north and south and aligned with an outer stellar oval reminiscent of a weak primary bar. Gravity torques The NUGA analysis continues by interpreting the distribution and kinematics of the gas, case-by case, in terms of feeding mechanisms. To do this, we compute the gravitational torques by first using high-resolution optical and near-infrared images of the galaxies to derive gravitational potential. We can then quantify the efficiency of the stellar potential to drain the gas angular momentum: negative torques mean that the gas is losing angular momentum and flowing into the central regions, while positive torques are driving the gas outwards. Figure torques shows the relative torques, dL/L , derived for NGC 2782 from the 3.6 m Spitzer /IRAC image. Both ^ 12 CO(1-0) and ^ 12 CO(2-1) emission give a similar picture: the average torques are negative in the inner few kpc of NGC 2782, down to the resolution limit of our images.

Figure rot-cur shows this comparison performed for NGC 2782. In this figure, one can identify the nuclear bar with _b 270 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 and corotation (CR) at 1.1 kpc, and the primary bar with _b 65 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 and an Inner Lindblad Resonance (ILR) at 1.3 kpc, very close to the CR of the nuclear bar.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2782 as a 34 Mpc merger remnant with prominent HI and stellar tidal tails, and studies young UV regions in its western HI tail.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage 2010 We have searched for young star-forming regions around the merger remnant NGC 2782. By using GALEX FUV and NUV imaging and HI data we found seven UV sources, located at distances greater than 26 kpc from the center of NGC 2782, and coinciding with its western HI tidal tail. These regions were resolved in several smaller systems when Gemini/GMOS -band images were used.

NGC 2782 (Arp 215) is at a distance of 34 Mpc (Smith 1994). Its heliocentric radial velocity is 2543 2 km s ^ 1 (from NED database). This galaxy shows a prominent tidal tail detected in HI, located to the western side of the object. A second tidal tail formed by a stellar component is seen to the east side of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper finds a central X-ray source in NGC 2782 with hot gas, a flat high-energy component, and significant 6.4 keV emission, making a hidden AGN plausible in its starburst system.

quotes.quote: NGC 2782 The X-ray source has 597 net counts. It can be seen in fig:2782spec that the extracted spectrum has a pronounced bump at energies below 1.5 keV which is well fitted by a hot diffuse-gas model. The high energy region is fitted by a power law which appears to be flat. The best fit (   =1.0 ) requires a gaussian component at 6.4 keV, (EW 1.5 keV) with a high-significance F statistic value of 17.0 and associated random probability 3 10^ 4 .

The galaxy is number 215 in Arp's Atlas of Peculiar Galaxies 1966ApJS...14....1A and has Hubble type classification SAB(rs)a pec 2005ApJ...618L..21W . Amongst nearby spirals, it harbours one of the most powerful, M 82-like, nuclear starbursts 1983ApJ...268..602B,1989ApJ...346..126D , fuelled by a massive, gas-rich nuclear bar, driving molecular gas towards the central regions at large gas-inflow rates, > 1 \ 1999ApJ...526..665J . A pair of prominent \ and optical tails surrounding an optical disk with three ripples 1991ApJ...378...39S,1999AJ....117.1237S suggest that the starburst may have been triggered by an interaction or merger 1994AJ....107.1695S .

sources.summary: UGC 4862 is treated as an interacting galaxy with two tidal tails, low outer H I density, swept-out gas, and enhanced outer-disk FUV star formation linked to an XUV disc.

quotes.quote: The very discrepant galaxy in the figures is the interacting galaxy UGC 4862 which has two tidal tails 2012MNRAS.421.3612T . The most prominent tail in the outer regions is masked in \ and FUV images when driving radial profils. The \ profile shows very low density in the outer regions, especially near the optical radius, compared to other galaxies.

The \ gas may be swept out toward the tail during interacting process, causing the low \ in the regions. On the other hand, the FUV profile shows much higher SFR near the radius of 2 \ than that of other galaxies. The high SFR in the outer regions might be linked to an extended UV (XUV) disc 2007ApJS..173..538T of this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 2782 as a counterexample: its western HI-rich tidal tail lacks CO, a bound TDG-sized condensation, and active H2-driven star formation.

quotes.quote: An interesting counterexample is the western tidal arm (or tail) of NGC 2782. It clearly stems from NGC 2782 and is very HI-rich with many condensations with average column densities of N _ HI 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 over regions several kpc in size Table 4 of Smith94b . The HI is accompanied by a weak cospatial stellar plume of surface brightness about _ B 25 Smith91b or perhaps slightly weaker from comparison with Jogee98 .

Neither we nor Smith99 detected CO despite the high HI column densities and the presence of disk stars. NGC 2782 has no single big (TDG-sized) HI condensation at the end of the western tidal tail and indeed the lack of CO provides a coherent picture: the HI here is not condensing because the tail is not gravitationally bound and thus H _2 is not forming so star formation has not started. In fact, the interaction has certainly added some energy to the tail so we expect that the clouds may separate further.

sources.summary: The paper recovers NGC 2782 in deeper VLBA observations, finding slight parsec-scale elongation resembling its kpc-scale VLA wing and radio luminosity near the detection upper-limit level.

quotes.quote: For the additional snapshot observation sample, the nuclei for both the detections (NGC 3147 and NGC 3516) appear unresolved, but with follow up higher sensitivity deep observations, the core for NGC 2782 and NGC 4388 appear slightly elongated.

For NGC 2782, a large kpc-scale wing-like structure primarily spread in the north-south direction in the VLA A-configuration https://public.nrao.edu/vla-configurations/ C-band image also exhibited a similar elongation in our VLBA deep observation on very small scales (pc, as compared with kpc) around the central core of the AGN.

Radio luminosities for the detections except for NGC 1068 and NGC 2782 all lie above this 5 rms line. NGC 1068 is one of the nearest sources from the initial snapshot sample (16.3 Mpc), so its proximity combined with the high sensitivity of the data and extended morphology enabled its detection despite its low radio luminosity. NGC 2782 is one of the more distant deep observations, and its radio luminosity is comparable to the maximum upper limit level. Both are considered to host AGN that are heavily obscured, with NGC 1068 especially so.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 2782 as a nuclear-bar case where molecular gas is being driven inward to feed a powerful central starburst as the large-scale bar dissolves.

quotes.quote: NGC 2782 provides a nice observational case of a nuclear bar driving gas down to 100 pc scales. It hosts a nuclear stellar bar which is associated with 2.5 10^9 M _ of molecular gas and appears to be channeling gas into the central 100 pc where an M82-class powerful central starburst resides (Fig. 10; Jogee, Kenney, Smith 1999). The nuclear stellar bar is identified via isophotal fits to K band image showing a characteristic plateau in position angle (PA) as the ellipticity rises to a maximum. The molecular gas distribution is bar-like, leads the nuclear stellar bar, and shows weak bar-like streaming motions (Jogee et al. 1999).
NGC 2782 may be witnessing the early decoupling phase of a nuclear stellar bar induced by gas inside the OILR of the large-scale bar, which itself appears to be dissolving.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 2782 as a peculiar spiral with a western H I plume and eastern stellar tail explained by an off-center collision about 200 Myr ago.

quotes.quote: One-sided HI tails and plumes NGC 2782 . --- Smith (1994) finds a 54 kpc long H i plume on the western side of the peculiar spiral galaxy NGC 2782, opposite to its eastern stellar tail. Using kinematic modelling she demonstrates that an off-center head-on collision between two galaxies of mass ratio 4:1 about 200 Myr ago can reproduce the observed system. In such a scenario, the smaller companion becomes the eastern extension and the long western plume consists of gas pulled out from the larger galaxy. Smith (1994) derives an H i mass of 1.4 10^9 M _ for the western plume, which is 40 of the system's total H i mass.

sources.summary: The paper derives NGC 2782 CO luminosity from a PdBI map, notes missing flux and 54 percent recovery, and uses the CO luminosity as a lower limit.

quotes.quote: Similarly, we measured L'_ CO of NGC 2782 using the PdBI CO (1--0) map 2008A&A...482..133H . 2014ApJS..214....1U have found that the data suffer from missing flux. The recovered flux is 54 . We thus use L'_ CO of NGC 2782 as the lower limit.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts NGC 2782 with NGC 4194, noting molecular gas at the eastern-tail base and apparent counterrotation of its tidal molecular material.

quotes.quote: The fact that the CO emission is detected far out in the actual optical tail sets it apart from the tidal CO detection in the minor (disk/disk) merger NGC 2782 --- even if the linear distance from the centre of the galaxy may be the same as that for our detection in NGC 4194. In NGC 2782, 6 10^8 M _ of molecular gas are associated with a depression in the optical emission at the base of the eastern tail (Smith \ 1999).
In contrast, the tidal molecular material of NGC 2782 (Smith \ 1999) is in apparent counterrotation to the central gas which underlines the difference in merger history between NGC 4194 and NGC 2782.

gmw_00002547.name: W2R 1926 + 42
gmw_00002547.overview: W2R 1926 + 42 is a blazar monitored for optical microvariability [arXiv:1611.06311, arXiv:1603.00140]. Its light curve shows rapid, asymmetric flare-like variations that likely originate from high-energy accelerated particles in the galactic jet [arXiv:1611.06311].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Kepler microvariability of blazar W2R 1926 + 42, deriving stacked shot profiles, rapid rise and decay timescales, and jet-particle explanations for asymmetry.

quotes.quote: We analyze the Kepler monitoring light curve of a blazar W2R 1926 + 42 to examine features of microvariability by means of the "shot analysis" technique. We select 195 intra-day, flare-like variations (shots) for the continuous light curve of Quarter 14 with a duration of 100 d. In the application of the shot analysis, an averaged profile of variations is assumed to converge with a universal profile which reflects a physical mechanism generating the microvariability in a blazar jet, although light-variation profiles of selected shots show a variety. A mean profile, which is obtained by aligning the peaks of the 195 shots, is composed of a spiky-shape shot component at 0.1 d (with respect to the time of the peak), and two slow varying components ranging from 0.50 d to 0.15 d and from 0.10 d to 0.45 d of the peak time. The former spiky feature is well represented by an exponential rise of 0.043 0.001 d and an exponential decay of 0.061 0.002 d.

These timescales are consistent with that corresponding to a break frequency of a power spectrum density calculated from the obtained light curve. After verification with the Monte-Carlo method, the exponential shape, but not the observed asymmetry, of the shot component can be explained by noise variation. The asymmetry is difficult to explain through a geometrical effect (i.e. changes of the geometry of the emitting region), but is more likely to be caused by the production and dissipation of high-energy accelerated particles in the jet.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes W2R 1926+42's Kepler optical lightcurve with the correlation integral method and finds no low-dimensional chaotic behavior, suggesting single-zone blazar emission models may be oversimplified.

quotes.quote: We search for low-dimensional chaotic signatures in the optical lightcurve of the Kepler field blazar W2R 1926+42. The frequently used correlation integral method is employed in our analysis. We find no apparent evidence for the presence of low-dimensional chaos in the lightcurve. If further confirmed, these results could be of importance for modeling the blazar emission mechanisms.

If further confirmed, our results (i.e. there is no low-dimensional behavior in the blazar lightcurves W2R 1926+42) indicate that the often invoked one-zone model may not correctly describe the blazar emission. The idea that synchrotron emission from a single emitting region and its temporal evolution should be controlled by a relatively small number of parameters, as the electron density distribution, magnetic field, Doppler factor, etc., cannot be confirmed by our analysis. Perhaps there are several, independently emitting/evolving regions that contribute to the blazar emission and thus the number of the governing variables can not be small.

gmw_00002548.name: HE 1503+0228
gmw_00002548.overview: HE 1503+0228 is a quasar host galaxy classified as a normal spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0208514]. Its stellar populations and gas ionization state are highly similar to those observed in normal, non-active galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0208514].

sources.summary: The paper deconvolves the HE 1503+0228 quasar host, finding normal non-AGN stellar populations and gas ionization, deriving velocity curves and masses, and concluding the host is a normal spiral galaxy.

quotes.quote: The stellar populations and gas ionization state of the host galaxy of are very similar to the ones measured for normal non-AGN galaxies. Dynamical information is also available for the gas and stellar components of the galaxy. Using deconvolution and a deprojection algorithm, velocity curves are derived for emission lines, from the center up to 4 away from the nucleus of the galaxy. Fitting a simple three-components mass model (point mass, spherical halo of dark matter, disk) to the position-velocity diagram, we infer a mass of M( < 1kpc) = (2.0 0.3) 10^ 10 M _ within the central kiloparsec of the galaxy, and a mass integrated over 10 kpc of M( < 10kpc) = (1.9 0.3) 10^ 11 M _ , with an additional 10 error due to the uncertainty on the inclination of the galaxy. This, in combination with the analysis of the stellar populations indicates that the host galaxy of is a normal spiral galaxy.

gmw_00002549.name: host galaxy of GRB 040827
aliases_external: host galaxy / NIR source XMM 2 of GRB 040827
gmw_00002549.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 040827 is a faint, extended galaxy that dominates the optical and near-infrared emission of the source [arXiv:astro-ph/0505261]. Photometric observations constrain the galaxy to a likely redshift between 0.5 and 1.7 [arXiv:astro-ph/0505261].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a faint host dominating the optical/NIR bands, measures its photometry, obtains a spectrum, and infers a redshifted gas column at roughly z = 0.5-1.7.

quotes.quote: In the optical/NIR range, the afterglow emission was observed in the K_ s band, as a weak source superimposed to the host galaxy, with magnitude K_ s = 19.44 0.13 (12 hours after the GRB, contribution from the host subtracted); in other bands the flux is dominated by the host galaxy. Coupling constraints derived from X-ray spectral fitting and from photometry of the host, we estimated a gas column density in the range (0.4-2.6) 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 in the GRB host galaxy, likely located at a redshift 0.5<z<1.7 .

No significant variation of the source was seen from 1.5 up to 30 days after the burst, indicating that the source was dominated by the host galaxy. This is consistent with the source being extended. In particular, there is no sign of afterglow emission in the R and I bands (for which we have not data during the first night). In our early J and H images, a contribution from the afterglow is likely present, but the host galaxy is contributing by a significant, unknown amount, hampering the determination of the intrinsic afterglow brightness. After averaging over the available measurements, we get for the host galaxy R = 23.10 0.12 , I = 22.57 0.06 and K_ s = 20.05 0.07 . Therefore, we can estimate the afterglow magnitude to be K_ s = 19.44 0.13 and K_ s > 21.02 (3 ) on 2004 Aug 27.98 and 2004 Aug 28.97 respectively. Assuming a power law decay ( F(t) t^ ), the decay index is constrained to be > 1.30 (3 ). We caution that this value is computed from just two measurements, and may be influenced by local deviations from the regular decay and/or measurement errors. This limit is consistent with the X-ray decay index. A spectrum of the host galaxy was acquired with FORS 1, starting on 2004 Aug 30.00 (2 hours exposure with the grism 300V).

gmw_00002550.name: host galaxy of GRB 060605
gmw_00002550.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 060605 is a very faint, extended source identified in deep optical imaging [arXiv:0804.2457]. It has an estimated magnitude of R_C = 26.4, though its physical association with the gamma-ray burst remains unconfirmed due to a lack of redshift data [arXiv:0804.2457].

sources.summary: The paper reports a faint extended possible host at the GRB afterglow position, estimates R_C = 26.4, and cautions that the weak detection lacks a confirming redshift.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy To search for the host galaxy of GRB 060605 and the potential foreground absorber detected in our optical spectrum (Fig. fig:doublez ) we used VLT/FORS2 imaging data of the field obtained under the ESO Large Programme 177.A-0591 (PI: Jens Hjorth). Based on images obtained with the 3.5m Italian Telescopio Nationale Galileo on La Palma (Malesani et al. 2006; J. Deng et al., in preparation) we were able to derive an improved astrometric position of the optical transient. Its refined position is R.A. (J2000) = 21 ^h 28 ^m 37 314 and Decl. = --06 ^ 3 30 88. On the deep VLT image the afterglow can be positioned with an accuracy of 0.1 arcsec. At this position a very faint extended source is visible (Fig. fors ). Using the average zeropoint of FORS2 R_C band images in the time period from July to September 2007 ( 28.404 0.037 ), as it is provided on ESO's web pages, we derive a magnitude of R_C=26.4 0.3 for this source (aperture diameter = 10 pixels).

If this is the host galaxy, it implies that at its peak time the optical afterglow of GRB 060605 was approximately 11 mag brighter in R_C than its host. We caution, however, that the detection is weak and we cannot claim that this object is the host of GRB 0606005 as we have no information about its redshift. On the other hand, its position underlying the optical transient and its faint magnitude make a potential physical association with GRB 060605 a reasonable assumption.

gmw_00002551.name: RIQ J0748+2200
gmw_00002551.overview: RIQ J0748+2200 is a radio-intermediate quasar and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0910.1487]. The source exhibits intranight optical variability, including a documented brightening event of 1.4 percent [arXiv:0910.1487].

sources.summary: The quasar was monitored over four nights; one possible variability event was seeing-contaminated, while another was accepted as intranight optical variability with no long-term change.

quotes.quote: RIQ J0748+2200: We monitored this RIQ on four nights spanning about a year. Formally significant variations with 2.3 and 1.4 , were seen on 23 Jan. 2007 and 30 Jan. 2008, respectively. Note that this RIQ has a contaminating nearby faint object 8 ^ .0 offset along PA 229 ^ . Still we have carried out aperture photometry, following the argument (Howell 1990) that for moderately clustered objects, i.e., those separated by 2 FWHM from the companion, the technique of optimum extraction based on aperture growth curve method can be taken as a viable alternative to the traditional crowded point source photometry.

A high statistical significance was also estimated for the INOV seen on 30 Jan. 2007, when the RIQ brightened by 1.4 (Fig. 1). Although in this case too, the brightening coincides with the time when the seeing changed from 2^ .0 to 2^ .5 (between UT 18.0 to 19.5 hours), we do not see any general correlation between the variations of seeing and the source magnitude and there is also a very high value of C_ eff = 5.1 for this night. Taking this into account we have designated this RIQ as variable (V) on this night (Table 2).

gmw_00002552.name: RIQ J0832+3707
gmw_00002552.overview: RIQ J0832+3707 is a radio-quiet Seyfert 1.2 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9705034] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1003.5700]. The object exhibits long-term variability in its broad, double-peaked H-alpha emission lines [arXiv:1003.5700] and has demonstrated internight optical variability [arXiv:0910.1487].

sources.summary: The paper monitors CBS 74 as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present results for seven objects whose long-term variability has not been studied in any detail: 3C 59, 1E 0450.3--1817, Pictor A, CBS 74, PKS 0921--213, PKS 1020--103, and PKS 1739+18C.
The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

In this paper we have characterized, in a model-independent way, the variability of the broad, double-peaked H emission lines in seven objects (Pictor A, PKS 0921--213, 1E 0450.3--1817, CBS 74, 3C 59, PKS 1739+18, and PKS 1020--103) which have been monitored over the past decade. As indicated by the rms profiles, the greatest variability occurs at larger projected velocities, and in particular many objects show a peak in the rms profile at large negative projected velocities. Difference spectra showed that the variability is caused primarily by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and amplitude on timescales of a few years and drift in projected velocity on longer timescales.

sources.summary: CBS 74 is confirmed as a radio-quiet Seyfert 1.2 galaxy with an unusually broad H-alpha component compared with most Seyferts.

quotes.quote: CBS 74 is a Seyfert galaxy according to Wagner et al. (1988). It was not detected at 4850 MHz by Gregory Condon (1991) (S < 25 mJy) and is therefore a radioquiet object. Our spectra (Fig. 3, 9) show that it is a Seyfert 1.2 galaxy with a very broad H component (FWHM 12000 kms ^ 1 ) and R=3.6. Such broad lines are common in radioloud quasars and broad line radiogalaxies (Miley Miller, 1979; Wills Browne, 1986; Brotherton et al., 1994; Eracleous Halpern, 1994), but they are rare in Seyfert galaxies although a few cases are known, such as 2E 0450-1816 (Eracleous Halpern, 1994) and Arp 102B (Chen Halpern, 1989).

sources.summary: The quasar was included despite being fainter than the nominal threshold, showed no intranight optical variability, but had the paper's clearest internight variability.

quotes.quote: Lastly, we note that the RIQ J0832 + 3707 is included in the our sample inspite of its being nearly 1-mag fainter than the adopted threshold ( M_ B < 23.5 mag), since it is found to be stellar in the FBQS survey (White et al. \ 2000).

RIQ J0832+3707: We monitored this RIQ on four nights spanning seven weeks. No INOV was detected; however, this RIQ remains thus far our best case of internight variability, showing a fading by 0.06 mag between 21 Feb. \ 2007 and 10 Mar. \ 2007 and a brightening of 0.02 mag by the following night (Fig. 2).

gmw_00002553.name: RIQ J1259+3423
gmw_00002553.overview: RIQ J1259+3423 is a radio-intermediate quasar [arXiv:0910.1487]. The active galaxy has shown probable intranight variability during monitoring, as well as internight variability of 0.2 magnitude over a 60-hour span [arXiv:0910.1487].

sources.summary: The quasar was classified as probably variable on one monitored night; earlier work found internight variability, while this paper found none over six days.

quotes.quote: RIQ J1259+3423: Out of the three nights we monitored this RIQ, it is classified as ``probable variable'' on the night of 20 Apr. 2007. Earlier, Carini et al. (2007) monitored it for four nights during 1998. While they did not detect significant intranight fluctuations, internight variability of 0.2 magnitude was detected by them over the course of 60 hours. In our observations spanning six days, this RIQ showed no internight variability.

gmw_00002554.name: RIQ J1336+1725
gmw_00002554.overview: RIQ J1336+1725 is a radio-intermediate quasar [arXiv:0910.1487]. Although historical observations identified the source as radio variable, optical monitoring over three years revealed no short-term variability and only a moderate longer-term fading of 0.03 magnitudes [arXiv:0910.1487].

sources.summary: The paper notes prior radio variability for the quasar, but its own three-night monitoring found no variability and only a modest longer-term optical fading.

quotes.quote: Falcke, Mathew Biermann (1995a) find J1336+1725 to be radio variable, but not J1701+5149.

RIQ J1336+1725: This RIQ remained non-variable on all the three nights it was monitored by us in the course of three years. In the longer term, it has shown a moderate fading by 0.03 mag over a year (Fig. 2).

gmw_00002555.name: RIQ J1719+4804
gmw_00002555.overview: RIQ J1719+4804 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1002.4681] with two associated absorption-line systems [arXiv:1307.6799]. The active nucleus exhibits strong accretion disk radiation [arXiv:1002.4681] and has demonstrated historical intranight optical variability [arXiv:0910.1487].

sources.summary: The paper treats PG 1718+481 as an FSQ outlier with strong accretion-disk radiation, high UV luminosity, low radio-to-UV index, and CIV blueward excess that may overwhelm jet-driven red-wing emission.

quotes.quote: The FSQ PG 1718+481 with A=-0.33 and the RQQ E1821+643 with A=0.26 . If the interpretation given is correct, then there should be evidence that PG 1718+481 has strong accretion disk radiation and E1821+643 has a powerful jet. The primary indicator that a luminous accretion flow (a quasar) is present in a galactic nucleus is the detection of a large UV peak in the spectral energy distribution sun89 . Thus, this first prediction seems to be verified by the fact that L_ (1350 )= 2 10^ 47 ergs/s for PG 1718+481, by far the largest value in Figure 2. This large UV flux accounts for the small value of _ 10 ^ UV =0.424 , one of the lowest in the RLQ sample.

The continuum flux is almost certainly from the accretion disk and not the high frequency tail of the synchrotron emission from the jet based on the following four facts. First, the spectral index is too flat from 3350 to 1650 , _ =0.76 to be a blazar synchrotron emission and the EWs of the BELs are too large for the continuum to be dominated by synchrotron emission sti91,wil95 . Also, the NASA Extragalactic Database notes that it is not optically variable and the optical polarization is low, both inconsistent with a synchrotron interpretation of the continuum. Within the two component model, the accretion disk luminosity is so strong the it induces (through a yet to be determined physical process) a large blueward excess which swamps any redwing contributions induced by the relativistic jet (through a yet to be determined process).

sources.summary: The quasar showed no intranight variability in the authors' monitoring, a later 0.04 mag brightening, and confirmed INOV in earlier Jang and Miller observations.

quotes.quote: RIQ J1719+4804: We monitored this RIQ on three nights over a month, but no INOV was detected. Likewise, internight change was also not found between 29 Apr. \ 2006 and the following night. However, a brightening by 0.04 mag was observed between 30 Apr. 2006 and 30 May 2006 (Fig. 2). Jang Miller (1995) monitored this RIQ on two nights during 1994, for durations of 3.3 and 3.8 hours. They report confirmed INOV on the former night (Table 2).

sources.summary: PG 1718+481 has two associated absorption systems in its E230M spectrum, one consistent with full coverage and another with two kinematic components.

quotes.quote: PG 1718+481 ( =1.0548, 1.0874) -- We detect two associated absorption-line systems in the E230M spectrum of this quasar. The =1.0548 \ is detected in Ly , , and the 1031.926, 1037.617 doublet (Figure fig:pg1718a ). The 1238.821, 1242.804 doublet is covered, but not detected. The doublet is consistent with full coverage. The =1.0874 \ is detected in Ly , , and the 1031.926, 1037.617 doublet.

gmw_00002556.name: RIQ J1312+3515
gmw_00002556.overview: RIQ J1312+3515 is a radio-intermediate flat-spectrum quasar [arXiv:2212.07061] hosted by a galaxy with tightly wrapped spiral arms [arXiv:0808.0918]. While the quasar shows a compact radio core [arXiv:2212.07061] and no intranight optical variability [arXiv:0910.1487], a 0.10 magnitude fading was observed over a two-year period [arXiv:0910.1487].

sources.summary: The paper describes PG1309+355 as a radio-intermediate flat-spectrum quasar with compact radio morphology, spiral-like host substructure, weak core polarization, and possible unresolved jet structure.

quotes.quote: PG1309+355 This radio-intermediate FSRQ ( aka Ton1565) has been suggested to be a boosted RQ quasar Miller1993 . It shows a compact radio morphology in VLA A and D array 5 GHz data Kellermann1994 . Its host galaxy shows evidence of a spiral-like substructure Kim2008 .

However, we detect only a compact radio core in our 5 GHz data (Figure fig8 ) which shows marginal fractional polarization ( 0.4 ). Our VLA data has failed to detect any extended diffuse emission or hotspots on kpc-scales. An archival VLA A-array image http://www.vla.nrao.edu/astro/archive/pipeline/position/J131217.7+351521/ at 22 GHz (resolution 100 75 mas) does indicate a faint jet-like extension to the west of the core.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1309+355 as a radio-intermediate quasar whose host is classified between spiral and elliptical, has tightly wrapped spiral arms, and shows old-population [OII] emission.

quotes.quote: PG 1309+355 PG 1309+355 (labeled 6 in our plots) could be called a radio-intermediate quasar. It falls into the category of radio-quiet using the kellermann94 cut at L _ 5 GHz 10 ^ 26 W Hz ^ 1 , but is classified as radio-loud using the criterion of radio-to-optical flux density ratio greater than 10 brinkmann97,yuan98,hamilton02 . The absolute magnitude of the quasar is M _ V = 24.4 and radio luminosity is L _ 5 GHz =1.8x10 ^ 41 erg s ^ 1 . The host galaxy, at M _ V = 22.8, is classified as an early-type spiral by bahcall97 and hamilton02 and as a moderately elongated elliptical by guyon06 . The HST images show tightly wrapped spiral arms.

sources.summary: The literature-monitored quasar showed no intranight optical variability, no reported four-day long-term fluctuations, but a separate program found a two-year fading of 0.10 mag.

quotes.quote: RIQ J1312+3515: This RIQ was monitored by Carini et al. (2007) for three nights but no INOV was detected (Table 2). They do not comment on any longer-term fluctuations over the four-days' time span covered by their observations. Although INOV remained undetected also in the three nights' monitoring of this RIQ by Sagar et al. (2004) (Table 2), a 0.10 mag fading was found between the first two epochs of their monitoring programme, separated by two years.

gmw_00002557.name: XMM3-3085
gmw_00002557.overview: XMM3-3085 is a candidate Lyman-break galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 10.85 [arXiv:1911.12832]. If confirmed as a high-redshift source, it would be an extremely luminous galaxy at its redshift [arXiv:1911.12832].

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM3-3085 as a z ~ 10.85 Lyman-break candidate whose photometry disfavors low-redshift or brown-dwarf fits and would make it extremely luminous if confirmed.

quotes.quote: We find one z 11 candidate that passes all of our selection criterion in the wide part of our survey. As shown in Fig. fig:z10 , for XMM3-3085 the low-redshift fit is unable to reproduce the near-infrared photometry. The ^2 distribution shows that a z 3 solution is the next most probable, and this solution is formally acceptable in the SED fitting analysis. The object is excluded as a brown dwarf based on the poor ^2 in our brown dwarf fitting.

The candidate LBG, XMM3-3085, has a photometric redshift of z_ phot = 10.85^ +1.00 _ 1.02 . If confirmed as a high-redshift source, this object would be the most luminous LBG known at z > 7 with a M_ UV 23.7 . The detection of one such source over our survey area however, is not unexpected from our evolving DPL model.

gmw_00002558.name: easternmost H i clump
aliases_external: NGC 3628 tidal-tail easternmost H I clump / TDG candidate
gmw_00002558.overview: The easternmost H I clump is a tidal dwarf galaxy candidate located in the tidal tail of NGC 3628 [arXiv:1303.5335]. It exhibits radio continuum emission indicative of recent star formation and hosts a detectable large-scale magnetic field [arXiv:1303.5335].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the easternmost H I clump in NGC 3628's tidal tail as a tidal dwarf galaxy candidate with radio continuum emission, recent star formation, and a detectable ordered magnetic field.

quotes.quote: There are nearly no signs of radio continuum emission from H i tails, apart from a weak source coincident with an H i clump located at R.A._ 2000 = 11^ h 23^ m 11^ s , Dec_ 2000 = +13^ 42' 30'' (flux of 5.6 1.0 mJy; it is marked with an arrow in Figs.  pi and nvsseff ). This source could be a tidal dwarf galaxy (TDG) candidate; details can be found in Sect.  TDG .

We calculated the spectral index using our 2.64 GHz and NVSS data and obtained a value of 0.7 0.3. This could indicate that the radio continuum emission is coming from a recent star--formation period, supporting our claim for a TDG candidate. The size of the TDG candidate was assumed to be 4--6 kpc, because Chromey et al. ( ) gave 5.1 kpc as the size of the stellar clump. Using the same method as in Sect.  field , we obtained a total magnetic field strength 3.3 0.5 G and a magnetic field energy density 4.5 1.5 10^ 13 erg/ cm^3 . These values are somewhat lower than for the TDG candidate found in Stephan's Quintet (Nikiel-Wroczy nski et al. in prep.), but still reasonable for a large-scale magnetic field: Niklas ( ) derived B_ TOT = 9 3 G as mean for the normal-sized spiral galaxies -- a value about three times higher.
